title: Sorting Motions by Class and Purpose
setting: You are the parliamentarian at a community association's regular meeting. The chair is new to the gavel and keeps asking you to classify each proposal and name its purpose.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The regular meeting will come to order. We have several items tonight, and since I'm new to the gavel I'll lean on our parliamentarian.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: I move that the Association contribute $500 to the neighborhood food bank.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Before I state that, help me classify it. What class of motion is this, and what does that class mean for when it can be made?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what class is this motion, and what does that class mean?
turns.gold: It is a main motion (an original main motion). A main motion is one whose introduction brings business before the assembly; it can be made only while no other motion is pending, and it ranks lowest in the order of precedence.

ref.quote: A main motion is a motion whose introduction brings business before the assembly. As already noted, a main motion can be made only when no other motion is pending, and it ranks lowest in the order of precedence of motions.

turns.speaker: Member B
turns.line: We've been over this for twenty minutes. I move the previous question.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Remind me what adopting the previous question actually does. What is its purpose?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what is the purpose of the Previous Question?
turns.gold: The Previous Question is a subsidiary motion whose purpose is to immediately close debate and further amendment on the pending question so it comes to an immediate vote. It is undebatable and requires a two-thirds vote.

ref.quote: If it is desired to close debate and amendment of a pending motion so that it will come to an immediate vote, this can be proposed by moving the Previous Question.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: Point taken, but honestly I can barely hear over the noise from the lobby, and there's a guest in the room while we're about to take up a confidential personnel matter.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) There's clearly an urgent problem affecting us right now. What is the name of the motion that lets a member raise that kind of pressing matter?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: name the motion for this purpose.
turns.gold: That is a Question of Privilege — the member would Raise a Question of Privilege. It is a privileged motion that lets a member interrupt pending business to state an urgent request or motion affecting a right or privilege of the assembly or of a member.

ref.quote: If a pressing situation is affecting a right or privilege of the assembly or of an individual member (for example, noise, inadequate ventilation, introduction of a confidential subject in the presence of guests, etc.), a member can Raise a Question of Privilege…, which permits him to interrupt pending business to state an urgent request or motion.

title: The Order of Precedence in Action
setting: You are the parliamentarian at a board meeting where several secondary motions have stacked up on a main motion and the chair has lost track of how they rank.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: We have a main motion to purchase new chairs, and now the motions are piling up faster than I can track them.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) People keep saying one motion 'takes precedence' over another and that a motion 'yields.' In plain terms, what do those mean, and which pending motion is the one we actually act on?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: explain 'takes precedence' and 'yields,' and identify the immediately pending question.
turns.gold: A motion 'takes precedence over' another when it can be made and, once stated by the chair, temporarily replaces the other as the immediately pending question; the lower motion 'yields' to it. The immediately pending question is the most recently stated motion not yet voted on, and it is the one taken up first.

ref.quote: If two motions “A” and “B” are related under rules of parliamentary procedure in such a way that motion “B” can be made while motion “A” is pending and, when stated by the chair, can thus temporarily replace “A” as the immediately pending question, motion “B” takes precedence over (or takes precedence of) motion “A,” and motion “A” yields to motion “B.”

turns.speaker: Member B
turns.line: Right now three things are pending: the main motion, a motion to postpone it to next month, and a motion to lay it on the table.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Which of those do we deal with first, and why?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: which pending motion is taken up first, by order of precedence?
turns.gold: Take up the highest-ranking motion first. Lay on the Table outranks Postpone to a Certain Time, which outranks the main motion, so put Lay on the Table first; if it fails you drop to Postpone, and the main motion is decided last. Ranking motions are voted on in the reverse of the order in which they were moved.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) When I'm unsure whether a new motion is even allowed while something else is pending, what's the quickest reliable way to check?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: how to quickly determine precedence and whether a motion is in order.
turns.gold: Use Chart I, the order-of-precedence chart (RONR pp. t3–t5). It lists the thirteen ranking motions in order and shows what each takes precedence over and yields to, so a motion is in order only if it ranks above the immediately pending motion.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: If we do postpone the main motion, what happens to my pending amendment — does it just vanish?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Good question. What becomes of the amendment if the main motion is postponed or otherwise set aside?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what happens to an adhering motion when the main question is set aside?
turns.gold: The amendment adheres to the main motion. Because it must be decided before the main question can be decided, it stays attached: when the main motion is postponed or temporarily disposed of, the amendment goes with it and remains pending, to be decided first when the main question is taken up again.

ref.quote: A subsidiary or incidental motion adhere to a main question if it is related to the main question in such a way that—once introduced—it must be decided before the main question can be decided. Adhering motions thus remain connected with the main question if that question is interrupted or temporarily disposed of, and remain to be decided first if and when the main question is taken up again.

title: Reading the Motion Tables
setting: You are the parliamentarian coaching a chair who wants to understand a main motion's properties and how to use the standard motion tables.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: A member has moved to adopt a budget for the year. Before I handle it, I want to be sure I understand this motion's basic properties.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) It's a main motion — what are its characteristics as to precedence: over what can it be moved, and what can be moved while it is pending?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: state the main motion's precedence characteristics.
turns.gold: A main motion takes precedence of nothing — it cannot be moved while any other question is pending — so it ranks lowest. While it is pending, any subsidiary motion, any privileged motion, and any applicable incidental motion can be moved, and the main motion yields to all of them.

ref.quote: Takes precedence of nothing—that is, it cannot be moved when any other question is pending. It yields to all subsidiary, all privileged, and all applicable incidental motions; that is, any subsidiary or privileged motion, and any incidental motion that is applicable in the particular case at the particular time, can be moved while a main motion is pending.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) You keep pointing me to the 'standard descriptive characteristics' in the tables. How many are there, and what are they for?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what are the standard descriptive characteristics and their purpose?
turns.gold: There are eight standard descriptive characteristics for each motion — covering precedence and yielding, applicability, whether it interrupts, whether it needs a second, is debatable, is amendable, the vote required, and whether it can be reconsidered. They state the key rules for each motion and serve as points of comparison with the main motion; Tables II and IV–VII summarize them.

ref.quote: Many of the most important rules governing the use of the individual motions described in this chapter reduce to eight standard descriptive characteristics. In addition to containing basic rules of procedure for each motion, these characteristics serve as points of comparison showing how the motion resembles or differs from a main motion.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) When a member is unsure of the exact words to use in making a particular motion, where do I send them for the correct form?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: where to find the proper wording/form of a motion.
turns.gold: Refer to Table III, the table of the proper form for making each motion. It gives the exact wording — how each motion is phrased when it is made — so the member can state the motion in its correct standard form.

title: Framing a Resolution the Right Way
setting: You are the parliamentarian assisting a society that wants to adopt a position and is arguing over how to word it.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Our committee wants to make a public statement, and members are throwing around different ways to word it.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: Last year we voted that the Society supports the riverfront cleanup. I move that we reaffirm that position tonight.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Is a motion to reaffirm our earlier position in order?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: rule on the motion to reaffirm (and on refrain/negative motions).
turns.gold: No — a motion to reaffirm is not in order. The earlier motion is still in effect, so reaffirming serves no useful purpose; it would collide with the rules for Amend Something Previously Adopted, and if it failed it would create an ambiguous situation. Likewise, avoid a motion merely to 'refrain' from acting or one worded as a negative.

ref.quote: Motions to “reaffirm” a position previously taken by adopting a motion or resolution are not in order. Such a motion serves no useful purpose because the original motion is still in effect; also, possible attempts to amend a motion to reaffirm would come into conflict with the rules for the motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted…; and if such a motion to reaffirm failed, it would create an ambiguous situation.

turns.speaker: Member B
turns.line: Fine — I'll move a fresh resolution instead, but I really want our reasons and background spelled out in it.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) How should we frame a resolution that includes that background — and is such background required?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: how to frame a resolution with background; is a preamble required?
turns.gold: Cast it as a resolution and put the background or reasons in a preamble — one or more 'Whereas' clauses placed before the resolving ('Resolved, That…') clauses. Neither rule nor custom requires a preamble; use one only when the background is genuinely needed. In consideration the preamble is amended last.

ref.quote: When special circumstances make it desirable to include a brief statement of background, the motion should be cast in the form of a resolution, with the background or reasons incorporated in a preamble that is placed before the resolving clauses.

title: Changing a Main Motion
setting: You are the parliamentarian at a club meeting where the maker of a motion wants to change it and members want to reshape it once it is on the floor.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: I move that the club spend up to $200 on the summer picnic.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: Actually, wait — before you state it, I'd like to make it $250 instead.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Can the maker just change his own motion like that? And what if I had already stated the question?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: may the maker modify the motion, before and after the chair states it?
turns.gold: Yes, at this point he may. Before the chair states the question, the maker still controls it and may accept or reject a suggested modification and change it freely. Once the chair has stated the question, the assembly — not the maker — controls the wording, and the maker can then modify only by unanimous consent (or by moving to Amend).

ref.quote: After a main motion has been made and before the question has been stated by the chair, any member can quickly rise and, with little or no explanatory comment, informally suggest one or more modifications in the motion, which at this point the maker can accept or reject as he wishes. … After the question has been stated by the chair—although the assembly, and not the maker of the motion, then has control over its wording—the maker can request unanimous consent to modify the motion.

expected: before the question has been stated

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Suppose members like the idea but want it changed in bigger ways once it's on the floor. What are all the ways the assembly can change or improve a pending main motion before we finally act?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: list the means of changing/improving a pending main motion.
turns.gold: Five courses are open: (1) before the chair states it, the maker accepts an informal modification; (2) after it is stated, the maker modifies it by unanimous consent; (3) the subsidiary motion to Amend (majority vote) changes the wording or substitutes a whole new text; (4) Commit or refer it to a committee for redrafting; and (5) a member urges its rejection (or moves to Postpone Indefinitely) and then offers a different main motion.

ref.quote: By means of the subsidiary motion to Amend…, members can propose changes to be made in the wording and, within limits, the meaning of a pending main motion before it is voted on. These amendments must be seconded, are debatable, and are adopted by a majority vote.

title: Amending a Main Motion
setting: Regular meeting of the Lakeside Community Association. A main motion, “that the Association purchase three new laptops for the office,” is pending and debate has just opened.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The pending motion is “that the Association purchase three new laptops for the office.” The floor is open to debate and amendment.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: I move to amend by inserting “and a printer” after “laptops.”

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) A member has moved that primary amendment. Before I proceed — is it properly before us, and how must it be handled?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the nature of this amendment and how it must be processed.
turns.gold: This is a primary amendment: it modifies the wording of the pending main motion before that motion is acted upon. It requires a second, is debatable because the main motion is debatable, and is adopted by a majority vote.

ref.quote: The subsidiary motion to Amend is a motion to modify the wording—and within certain limits the meaning—of a pending motion before the pending motion itself is acted upon.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: It is moved and seconded to insert “and a printer.”

turns.speaker: Member B
turns.line: I move to amend that amendment by striking “a printer” and inserting “two printers.”

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) What do I call Member B’s motion, and is that the correct term?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the proper term for Member B's motion.
turns.gold: Member B has moved a secondary amendment — an amendment of the second degree. Both “secondary amendment” and “amendment to the amendment” are correct, but “secondary amendment” is the preferred term.

ref.quote: The terms amendment of the first degree and amendment of the second degree, or amendment to the main question and amendment to the amendment, are correct expressions, but the terms primary amendment and secondary amendment are preferred.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) The main motion to purchase equipment would itself take only a majority. What vote does the amendment need?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the vote required to adopt the amendment.
turns.gold: The amendment requires a majority vote, regardless of the vote that would be required to adopt the main motion being amended.

ref.quote: Requires a majority vote, regardless of the vote required to adopt the question to be amended.

title: Degrees and Series of Amendments
setting: A club meeting. A primary amendment to a resolution is pending, and a secondary amendment to that primary amendment is also pending.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Currently pending are the resolution, a primary amendment, and a secondary amendment to that primary amendment.

turns.speaker: Member C
turns.line: I move to amend the secondary amendment by adding the word “annually.”

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) May I entertain Member C’s motion to change the secondary amendment?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair whether Member C's motion is in order.
turns.gold: No. Member C is attempting an amendment of the third degree, which is not permitted. Rule the motion out of order; the member may instead state that if the secondary amendment fails, he will offer a different secondary amendment in its place.

ref.quote: An amendment of the third degree is not permitted. To accomplish the same purpose, a member can say, while a secondary amendment is pending, that if it is voted down, he will offer another secondary amendment—which he can then indicate briefly—in its place.

turns.speaker: Member D
turns.line: On the main resolution, I move three separate wording changes, all in one motion to amend.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Member D bundled three changes into one motion. Must the assembly vote on them together?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on how a series of amendments offered in one motion is handled.
turns.gold: A series of amendments may be offered in a single motion to Amend, but unless they are conforming amendments, any member may demand a separate vote on one or more of them. Ask whether any member demands a separate vote before proceeding.

ref.quote: A series of amendments to a pending main motion (or to a lengthy primary amendment, such as a substitute) may be offered in one subsidiary motion to Amend. Unless these amendments meet the standard for conforming amendments described immediately below, any member may demand a separate vote on one or more of them.

title: Is It Germane?
setting: City resident advisory council. Pending is a motion “that the council commend Officer George for his action in the incident of June 1.”

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Pending is the motion to commend Officer George for his action.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: I move to amend by striking out “commend” and inserting “censure.”

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) An amendment to reverse the sentiment entirely — is that germane and in order?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair whether the strike-and-insert amendment is germane.
turns.gold: It is germane and in order. Both “commend” and “censure” deal with the council’s opinion of the officer’s action, and an amendment may be hostile to — even defeat — the original motion’s spirit and still be germane, so long as it does not introduce an independent question.

ref.quote: An amendment cannot introduce an independent question; but an amendment can be hostile to, or even defeat, the spirit of the original motion and still be germane.

turns.speaker: Member B
turns.line: Then I move to amend by inserting “not” so it reads “that the council not commend Officer George.”

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Is that insertion of “not” in order?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair whether inserting “not” is a proper amendment.
turns.gold: No — rule it out of order. Merely inserting “not” makes adoption of the amended motion equivalent to a rejection of the original motion, which is an improper amendment.

ref.quote: The following types of amendment are not in order: 1) One that is not germane to the question to be amended. 2) One that merely makes the adoption of the amended question equivalent to a rejection of the original motion. Thus, in the motion that “our delegates be instructed to vote in favor of the increase in Federation dues,” an amendment to insert “not” before “be” is not in order because an affirmative vote on not giving a certain instruction is identical with a negative vote on giving the same instruction. But it would be in order to move to insert “not” before “to” (“instructed not to vote in favor”), since this would change the main motion into one to give different instructions. 3) One that would cause the question as amended to not be in order.

title: Preamble and the Same-Question Principle
setting: Annual meeting. A resolution with three “Whereas” clauses and two resolving clauses is under consideration.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: While the resolving clauses are still pending, I move to amend the second Whereas clause.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) May the assembly amend a Whereas clause now?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on when the preamble may be amended.
turns.gold: Not yet. The preamble is not opened to amendment until amendment of the resolving clauses has been completed. After any preamble amendment, a single vote is taken on adopting the entire resolution.

ref.quote: When a resolution has a preamble (one or more clauses beginning “Whereas”), the preamble is not opened to amendment until after amendment of the resolving clauses has been completed. After any amendment of the preamble, a single vote is taken on the question of adopting the entire resolution or paper.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The assembly has just voted to strike the words “by December 31” from the first resolving clause.

turns.speaker: Member B
turns.line: I move to amend by inserting “by December 31” back into that same clause.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) The assembly just removed those very words. May we entertain this?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair whether the new amendment may be entertained.
turns.gold: No. Once the assembly has voted that certain words shall not form part of the resolution, it is not in order during the same session to make another amendment raising the same question of content and effect. Rule it out of order.

ref.quote: After the assembly has voted that certain words (or a certain paragraph) shall, or shall not, form part of a pending resolution, it is not in order, during the same session at which that vote was taken, to make another motion to Amend that raises the same question of content and effect.

title: Forms of Amendment and Friendly Amendments
setting: Board meeting. A motion is pending and members are proposing changes.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: I move to insert the words “and the treasurer” — somewhere in there.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) The member didn’t say where. Can I accept this motion to insert as stated?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on what a motion to insert words must specify.
turns.gold: A motion to insert must specify the exact place of insertion — the word before or after which, or the words between which, the insertion is made. Ask the member to name the precise location before the motion can be stated.

ref.quote: A motion to insert words must specify the exact place of insertion by naming the word before or after which, or the words between which, the insertion is to be made—whichever will better locate and point out the effect of the change.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: The maker says she accepts my change as a friendly amendment, so let’s just move on.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) The maker “accepts” it. Do we still need to act on it?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on how to handle the accepted “friendly” amendment.
turns.gold: Yes. Regardless of whether the maker “accepts” it, the amendment must be opened to debate and voted on formally — unless adopted by unanimous consent — and is handled under the ordinary rules for amendments.

ref.quote: Regardless of whether or not the maker of the main motion “accepts” the amendment, it must be opened to debate and voted on formally (unless adopted by unanimous consent) and is handled under the same rules as amendments generally.

title: Stating Amendments and Filling Blanks
setting: Committee-of-the-whole style meeting. Several amendments are pending on a spending resolution, and members are losing track.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: I’m confused — which motion are we actually voting on right now?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) How do I keep this series clear as I state and put the questions?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the duty when stating and putting motions in a series.
turns.gold: In stating each question, and again when putting it to a vote, take care that members understand which motion is under immediate consideration and the effect of adopting or rejecting it.

ref.quote: In stating the question on each motion in any series involving amendments, and again when putting the motion to a vote, the chair should take care that the members understand which motion is under immediate consideration, as well as the effect of adopting or rejecting it.

turns.speaker: Member B
turns.line: Rather than fight over amendments, I move that we create a blank for the dollar amount.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) What exactly does filling a blank do for us here?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the nature of filling blanks.
turns.gold: Filling blanks is a device, related to amendment, that lets an unlimited number of alternative choices for a specification be pending at once — members can weigh all options before voting, rather than being limited to the ordinary three pending amendments.

ref.quote: Filling blanks, although not a form of amendment in itself, is a closely related device by which an unlimited number of alternative choices for a particular specification in a main motion or primary amendment can be pending at the same time.

turns.speaker: Member B
turns.line: So a dollar figure is a fair thing to put in a blank?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Is a monetary amount an appropriate case for filling a blank?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair whether a dollar amount is suited to filling a blank.
turns.gold: Yes. Cases adapted to filling blanks include motions containing names of persons or places, dates, numbers, or amounts — so a dollar figure is exactly the kind of specification suited to a blank.

ref.quote: Among cases adapted to such treatment are main motions or primary amendments containing names of persons or places, dates, numbers, or amounts.

title: Referring a Question to Committee
setting: Monthly membership meeting. Business is being conducted item by item.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: While no motion is pending, I move that the matter of a new website be referred to a committee of three.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Is that the subsidiary motion to Commit?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the nature of this motion.
turns.gold: No. Because no question is pending, this is not the subsidiary motion to Commit but a main motion — an original main motion, since the website is a new subject. Handle it as a main motion.

ref.quote: When a motion proposes to assign a task or refer a matter to a committee when no question is pending, such a motion is not the subsidiary motion to Commit, but is a main motion.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Later, a main motion is pending with a primary amendment and a motion to Postpone Indefinitely also pending.

turns.speaker: Member B
turns.line: I move to refer the main motion to the Finance Committee.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) If we commit this, what happens to the pending Postpone Indefinitely and the pending amendment?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the effect of committing on the adhering motions.
turns.gold: The pending motion to Postpone Indefinitely is dropped from further consideration. The pending amendment, however, adheres: it goes to the committee with the main motion and is reported back with it.

ref.quote: If a motion to Postpone Indefinitely is pending when the main motion is referred to a committee, the motion for indefinite postponement is dropped from further consideration. On the other hand, any pending amendments, motions for Division of a Question or Consideration by Paragraph or Seriatim, and adhering debatable appeals (or points of order submitted to the judgment of the assembly) go to the committee with the main motion, and are reported with it.

turns.speaker: Member C
turns.line: On the motion to commit, I’d like to argue why the underlying proposal is a bad idea.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) How far may debate on the motion to Commit go?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the permissible scope of debate on Commit.
turns.gold: Debate on Commit is limited to the desirability of committing and the appropriate details of the referral; it may not go into the merits of the main question itself. Confine the member accordingly.

ref.quote: Is debatable. The debate can extend only to the desirability of committing the main question and to the appropriate details of the motion to Commit, as explained below, however, and not to the merits of the main question.

title: Postponing to a Certain Time
setting: Society that holds regular weekly business meetings. Business is in progress.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: While no question is pending, I move to postpone the hearing of the building committee’s report.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Is that the subsidiary motion to Postpone?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the nature of this motion.
turns.gold: No. The report is not pending, so this is not the subsidiary motion to Postpone but an incidental main motion, handled under the rules for main motions.

ref.quote: When a motion proposes to postpone a matter that is not pending—for example, the hearing of a committee’s report—such a motion is not the subsidiary motion to Postpone, but is an incidental main motion.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: A main motion, under a two-minute-per-speaker limit adopted earlier, is postponed to next week’s meeting. That meeting has now arrived.

turns.speaker: Member B
turns.line: Are we still held to the two-minute speaking limit on this question?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Does the debate limit adopted last session still apply?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on whether prior debate-limiting orders carry to the later session.
turns.gold: No. When a postponed question is resumed at a later session, any orders limiting debate or ordering the Previous Question adopted at the original session are exhausted, and debate proceeds under the regular rules.

ref.quote: When consideration of a postponed question is resumed at a later session, any orders limiting or extending the limits of debate or for the Previous Question that were adopted at the original session are exhausted, and debate takes place according to the regular rules.

turns.speaker: Member C
turns.line: I move to postpone this to next week and make it a special order.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) What vote does that require?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the vote required to postpone.
turns.gold: A simple postponement requires only a majority. But because this motion would make the question a special order, it requires a two-thirds vote, since it suspends any rules interfering with taking the question up at the specified time.

ref.quote: Requires a majority vote in its simple and usual form. If (as originally moved or as a result of amendment) it makes a question a special order, however, the motion to Postpone then requires a two-thirds vote, because it suspends any rules that will interfere with the question’s consideration at the time specified.

title: Limits on Postponement and Special Orders
setting: Local society that holds regular business meetings on the same day each week.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: I move to postpone this question for two months.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Can we postpone it that far out?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the limit on the time to which a question may be postponed.
turns.gold: No. In a body meeting within a quarterly interval, a question may be postponed only until — but not beyond — the next regular business session, which for this weekly society is next week. Two months is out of order.

ref.quote: In a case where more than a quarterly time interval will elapse between meetings (for example, in an annual convention of delegates or in a local society that holds only an annual meeting), a question cannot be postponed beyond the end of the present session. In cases where no more than a quarterly time interval will elapse between sessions, a question can be postponed until, but not beyond, the next regular business session.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: Then I want it postponed to next week in a way that guarantees it comes up even if other business is running long.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) How can the member secure that priority, and what vote is needed?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on postponing so the question can interrupt other business.
turns.gold: Move to postpone and make the question a special order. That gives it priority to interrupt the consideration of other business at the specified time, and it requires a two-thirds vote for adoption.

ref.quote: To give the question being postponed a special type of priority that will enable it to interrupt the consideration of other business at the specified time, the motion to Postpone may, by a two-thirds vote, be adopted in a form that makes the question a special order.

title: Limiting and Extending Debate
setting: Association meeting where debate on a controversial motion has grown long.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: I move that debate on this motion be limited to two minutes per speaker.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) What are the ways this motion can operate, and can it force us to vote right now?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on how Limit or Extend Limits of Debate may limit debate.
turns.gold: It can limit debate two ways: by reducing the number or length of speeches, or by requiring that debate close at a set later hour or after a specified time. It cannot force an immediate close — that requires the Previous Question.

ref.quote: The motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate can limit debate by: (1) reducing the number or length of speeches permitted, without including specific provision for closing debate; or (2) requiring that, at a certain later hour or after debate for a specified length of time, debate shall be closed.

turns.speaker: Member B
turns.line: If we adopt this, when does the limit stop applying?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) What do we call this action, and when does it end?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the meaning of “order” and “exhausted.”
turns.gold: Adopting this motion is said to adopt an “order.” When that order finally ceases to be in force as to all the motions it affected, it is said to be “exhausted.”

ref.quote: When an order limiting or extending the limits of debate finally ceases to be in force as relates to all the motions it affected, the order is said to be “exhausted.”

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) What vote does the motion to limit debate require?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the vote required.
turns.gold: It requires a two-thirds vote, because it suspends the rules and takes away members’ basic right to full debate.

ref.quote: Requires a two-thirds vote—because it suspends the rules, and because limiting debate takes away the basic rights of all members to full discussion and may restrict a minority’s right to present its case.

title: Debate Limits on a Series; Superseding an Order
setting: Meeting with a resolution, a pending amendment, and a pending motion to Commit all before the assembly.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: I move that debate be limited to three minutes per speaker.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) The member didn’t say which of the pending questions this applies to. What is affected?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the scope of an unspecified motion to limit debate on a series.
turns.gold: When several debatable questions are pending, the motion could apply to the immediately pending question, a consecutive part of the series, or the whole series — but if it does not specify, only the immediately pending question is affected.

ref.quote: If, at the time the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is made, the immediately pending question is one among a consecutive series of pending debatable questions, the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate can be applied, as noted in Standard Characteristic 2 above, to the immediately pending question, to any consecutive part of the series beginning with the immediately pending question, or to the entire series. If the motion does not specify to which of these questions it is to apply, then only the immediately pending question is affected.

turns.speaker: Member B
turns.line: That three-minute limit is already in force, but I now move that debate instead be limited to one speech of five minutes each.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Can a second, conflicting limit be moved while the first is in effect?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair whether the new, conflicting limitation is in order.
turns.gold: Yes. Unlike main and lower subsidiary motions, adopting one order limiting debate does not bar a conflicting one; a motion to set different limits may be made until the affected questions are finally disposed of, because the two-thirds vote it needs also satisfies the requirement for suspending the rules.

ref.quote: A motion to set different limitation(s) or extension(s), or to change from one to the other, or to order the Previous Question, can be made at any time that it is in order under the order of precedence of motions, until the pending questions affected have been finally disposed of.

title: Ordering the Previous Question
setting: Meeting where a single main motion is under debate.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: I move the previous question.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) To what can this motion be made to apply?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the possible applications of the Previous Question.
turns.gold: The Previous Question can be applied to the immediately pending motion, to an entire series of pending debatable or amendable motions, or to any consecutive part of that series beginning with the immediately pending question. Made unqualified, as here, it applies only to the immediately pending motion.

ref.quote: Can be applied to any immediately pending debatable or amendable motion; to an entire series of pending debatable or amendable motions; and to any consecutive part of such a series, beginning with the immediately pending question.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) If it is ordered, what exactly is the effect?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the effect of adopting the Previous Question.
turns.gold: Adopting it immediately closes debate on, and stops amendment of, the motions it covers, and it bars any further subsidiary motions except the higher-ranking Lay on the Table.

ref.quote: Adopting or “ordering” the Previous Question: 1) immediately closes debate on, and stops amendment of, the immediately pending question and such other pending questions as the motion may specify (in consecutive series; see Standard Characteristic 2); and 2) prevents the making of any other subsidiary motions except the higher-ranking Lay on the Table.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) What vote is needed to order it?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the vote required.
turns.gold: The Previous Question requires a two-thirds vote. If it fails to reach two-thirds, debate simply continues as if the motion had not been made.

ref.quote: Requires a two-thirds vote. (If a motion for the Previous Question fails to gain the necessary two-thirds vote, debate continues as if this motion had not been made.)

title: Calls for the Question and Executed Orders
setting: Meeting where debate is heated; several motions are pending in series.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: Question! Question! Let’s just vote!

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Is a shouted “question” just noise, or does it mean something procedurally?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on how to treat the call for the question.
turns.gold: A “call for the question” is simply the Previous Question in nonstandard form. It is subject to all the same rules — it needs a second and a two-thirds vote and cannot cut off a member who still wishes to speak.

ref.quote: A motion such as “I call for [or “call”] the question,” “I demand the previous question,” “I move to close [or “end”] debate,” or “I move we vote now” is simply a motion for the Previous Question made in nonstandard form, and it is subject to all of the rules in this section.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The Previous Question was ordered on three motions. We have voted on one of them. A member now wants to reconsider the order for the Previous Question.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Is the order “partly executed,” and does that matter?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on partly executed versus fully executed orders.
turns.gold: Because one but not all of the three motions has been voted on, the order is partly executed — it is fully executed only when all covered motions have been voted on. An affirmative vote for the Previous Question cannot be reconsidered once the order is partly executed.

ref.quote: When the Previous Question has been ordered on a number of motions, the order is said to be partly executed (or partly carried out) if one or more, but not all, of these motions have been voted on. When all of the motions specified in the order have been voted on, it is fully executed.

expected: one or more but not all

turns.speaker: Member B
turns.line: I appeal from the chair’s ruling, and I’d like to speak at length on my appeal.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) The Previous Question order is still unexhausted. May this appeal be debated?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the effect of the unexhausted order on the appeal.
turns.gold: No. An appeal made after the Previous Question has been ordered and before that order is exhausted is undebatable. Put the appeal to a vote without debate.

ref.quote: An appeal is undebatable if it is made after the Previous Question has been moved or ordered and before the order is exhausted.

title: Taking a Recess
setting: Meeting where ballots must be counted and a short pause is needed.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: I’d like a brief pause. Someone suggested I just let the assembly “stand at ease” instead of recessing.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) What is the difference between standing at ease and a recess?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on recess versus standing at ease.
turns.gold: Standing at ease is a brief pause the chair permits without objection and without declaring a recess; there is technically no interruption and members stay in their places. A recess, by contrast, is a formal intermission taken by the assembly.

ref.quote: A meeting is said to stand at ease if the chair, without objection, simply permits a brief pause, without a declaration of recess. In such a case there is technically no interruption of the meeting, and members remain in their places.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) And what exactly is a recess?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the meaning of a recess.
turns.gold: A recess is a short intermission — often just a few minutes — that does not close the meeting; when it ends, business resumes at exactly the point where it was interrupted.

ref.quote: A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: No question is pending, but I move that we take a recess at 3 p.m.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) With nothing pending, is that the privileged motion to Recess?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on whether this is the privileged motion to Recess.
turns.gold: No. With no question pending, a motion to recess is a main motion, and the privileged-motion characteristics do not apply. A recess motion is privileged only while another question is pending; a recess at a future time is in order only when nothing is pending.

ref.quote: A motion to recess that is made when no question is pending (whether the recess is to begin immediately or at a future time) is a main motion, and the eight characteristics given below do not apply to it. Consequently, a motion to recess is privileged only when another question is pending; and a motion to take a recess at a future time is in order only when no question is pending.

title: Recess Characteristics and a Scheduled Recess
setting: Convention meeting following an adopted program that provides for a recess.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: A motion to recess is pending. I move to amend it to make the recess fifteen minutes instead of ten.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Is the recess motion amendable, and what vote does it take?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on amendability and the vote for the motion to Recess.
turns.gold: Yes, the privileged motion to Recess is amendable as to the length of the recess, and any such amendment is undebatable. The motion itself requires only a majority vote.

ref.quote: Is amendable as to the length of the recess; any such amendment is undebatable. 7. Requires a majority vote.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The adopted program calls for a recess at 3:00 p.m., and it is now 3:00.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Do I need a motion and vote to take this scheduled recess?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on declaring a recess already provided for in the program.
turns.gold: No. When a recess is provided for in the adopted agenda or program, the chair, without further action by the assembly, announces the fact and declares the assembly in recess when the time arrives.

ref.quote: If a recess is provided for in the adopted agenda or program of a convention or other meeting, the chair, without further action by the assembly, announces the fact and simply declares the assembly in recess when the specified time arrives.

title: The Motion to Adjourn
setting: Regular meeting of an ordinary society that has several regular meetings a year and no fixed hour of adjournment.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: I move to adjourn.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) What does it mean to adjourn, and what kind of motion is this?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the meaning of adjourn.
turns.gold: To adjourn means to close the meeting. A motion to adjourn may be either privileged or a main motion depending on the conditions; the common unqualified motion in an ordinary society is the privileged form.

ref.quote: To adjourn means to close the meeting. A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.

turns.speaker: Member B
turns.line: There’s no business pending — you can’t entertain an adjournment motion right now.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Is the motion in order even though nothing is pending?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the privileged motion to Adjourn when no question is pending.
turns.gold: It is in order. Whether or not business is pending, a majority should not be forced to stay in session longer than it wishes, and even with nothing pending, deciding whether to close should not be allowed to consume time — so the unqualified motion to adjourn is in order.

ref.quote: In such a case, regardless of whether business is pending, a majority should not be forced to continue in session substantially longer than it desires, and even if no business is pending, a decision as to whether to close the meeting should not be allowed to consume time.

turns.speaker: Member C
turns.line: I move that we adjourn at 9 p.m.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Is that qualified motion also privileged?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on whether the qualified motion to adjourn is privileged.
turns.gold: No. A motion to adjourn is privileged except when it is qualified — as this one is, fixing a future time. A qualified motion to adjourn is not privileged; it is treated as an incidental main motion and is out of order while business is pending.

ref.quote: A motion to adjourn is always a privileged motion except in the following cases: 1) When the motion is qualified in any way, as in the case of a motion to adjourn at, or to, a future time. 2) When a time for adjourning is already established, either because the assembly has adopted a motion or a program setting such a time, or because the order of business, the bylaws, or other governing rules prescribe it. 3) When the effect of the motion to adjourn, if adopted, would be to dissolve the assembly with no provision for another meeting, as is usually the case in a mass meeting or the last meeting of a convention.

title: Adjournment: Characteristics, Steps, and a Scheduled Hour
setting: Convention session following an adopted program that fixes an hour for adjournment.

turns.speaker: Member A
turns.line: The privileged motion to adjourn is pending. I’d like to speak against adjourning and offer an amendment to it.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) May the privileged motion to Adjourn be debated or amended, and what vote does it need?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the standard characteristics of the privileged motion to Adjourn.
turns.gold: No. The privileged motion to Adjourn is not debatable and not amendable, and it requires only a majority vote. Rule the member’s debate and amendment out of order.

ref.quote: Is out of order when another has the floor. 4. Must be seconded. 5. Is not debatable. 6. Is not amendable. 7. Requires a majority vote.

turns.speaker: Member B
turns.line: We’ve voted to adjourn but you haven’t declared it — may I still give notice of a motion for tomorrow?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) The vote to adjourn carried but I haven’t declared the meeting adjourned. What may still be done?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on what is in order after the vote to adjourn but before it is declared.
turns.gold: After it has been voted to adjourn but before you declare the meeting adjourned, it is still in order to take steps such as giving previous notice, making important announcements, or making (but not taking up) a motion to reconsider. Permit the member to give notice.

ref.quote: After it has been voted to adjourn but before the chair has declared the meeting adjourned, it is still in order to take any of the steps listed above, if necessary.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The program fixes adjournment at 5:00 p.m., and the clock has just reached 5:00.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Do I need a motion to adjourn now?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on adjourning at a scheduled hour.
turns.gold: No. When an hour for adjourning has been scheduled in the program or by adopted motion, no motion to adjourn is necessary; you simply announce the fact and declare the meeting adjourned. If the assembly does not wish to adjourn, it is handled as setting aside the orders of the day.

ref.quote: If an hour for adjourning a meeting within a convention or other session of more than one meeting has been scheduled—either in an agenda or program or by the adoption of a motion setting a time—no motion to adjourn is necessary when that hour arrives.

title: Points of Order at the Ridgeline Civic League
setting: A regular monthly meeting of the Ridgeline Civic League. A resolution on street lighting is being handled and several members watch the rules closely. You are seated beside the chair as parliamentarian.

turns.speaker: Member (Alvarez)
turns.line: Point of order! The chair just moved on to debate, but I never heard anyone second the motion on the street-lighting resolution.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The member has risen to a point of order concerning a missing second.
turns.prompt: The chair turns to you: what is a point of order for, and how should the chair handle Mr. Alvarez raising it here?
turns.gold: A point of order is the device by which any member who notices a breach of the rules calls on the chair to enforce them. Because the chair overlooked the missing second, Mr. Alvarez is right to insist on enforcement, and the chair should now rule on whether the point is well taken, stating brief reasons. Any two members may then appeal that ruling.

ref.quote: It is the right of every member who notices a breach of the rules to insist on their enforcement. If the chair notices a breach, he corrects the matter immediately; but if he fails to do so—through oversight or otherwise—any member can make the appropriate Point of Order.

turns.speaker: Member (Boone)
turns.line: Now that we've debated the lighting resolution for ten minutes, I'd like to raise a point of order that the motion was never properly seconded back at the start.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Mr. Boone raises the same second-related objection, but only now, after extended debate.
turns.prompt: The chair asks you: is Mr. Boone's point of order timely at this stage?
turns.gold: No. A point of order must be raised promptly at the time the breach occurs; the time to object to a missing second was when the chair stated the question. Once debate on the motion has begun, a point of order on that defect is too late, so the chair should rule Mr. Boone's point not well taken.

ref.quote: The general rule is that if a question of order is to be raised, it must be raised promptly at the time the breach occurs. For example, if the chair is stating the question on a motion that has not been seconded, or on a motion that is not in order in the existing parliamentary situation, the time to raise these points of order is when the chair states the motion. After debate on such a motion has begun—no matter how clear it is that the chair should not have stated the question on the motion—a point of order is too late.

turns.speaker: Member (Cho)
turns.line: Point of order. I've just realized the motion we adopted at last month's meeting directly conflicts with our bylaws. I know that was weeks ago, but surely something can be done?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Ms. Cho raises a point of order about an adopted motion that conflicts with the bylaws, well after the fact.
turns.prompt: The chair turns to you: is her point of order barred as untimely, or is it in order?
turns.gold: Her point of order is in order despite the delay. A motion adopted in conflict with the bylaws is a breach of a continuing nature, making the action null and void, and a point of order against such a breach may be raised at any time while the action has continuing force and effect. The chair should entertain the point on its merits.

ref.quote: The only exceptions to the requirement that a point of order must be made promptly at the time of the breach arise in connection with breaches that are of a continuing nature, whereby the action taken in violation of the rules is null and void. In such cases, a point of order can be made at any time during the continuance of the breach—that is, at any time that the action has continuing force and effect—regardless of how much time has elapsed.

title: Handling and Precedent of a Point of Order
setting: The Delta Watershed Council is midway through debate. A member is speaking when another interrupts. You advise the chair on the mechanics of the point of order and on the weight of the chair's rulings.

turns.speaker: Member (Fenn)
turns.line: Point of order! The speaker's remarks about the treasurer's honesty are out of order as a personal attack—and I demand a vote of the assembly on it right now, with debate.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Mr. Fenn interrupted the speaker with a point of order and is demanding a seconded, debatable vote on it.
turns.prompt: The chair asks you: what are the handling characteristics of his point of order—second, debate, amendment, and who decides?
turns.gold: A point of order requires no second and is not amendable, and it is normally ruled upon by the chair rather than voted on. It is not debatable, though the chair may permit brief explanation, and it is in order even though it interrupted the speaker. No vote is taken unless the chair is in doubt or the ruling is appealed, so Mr. Fenn is not entitled to demand a debatable vote.

ref.quote: 3. Is in order when another has the floor, even interrupting a person speaking or reading a report if the point genuinely requires attention at such a time (see Timeliness Requirement for a Point of Order, 23:5). 4. Does not require a second. 5. Is not debatable—but, with the chair’s consent, a member may be permitted to explain his point and knowledgeable or interested members can be heard by way of explanation. If the chair submits the point to a vote of the assembly, the rules governing its debatability are the same as for an Appeal (see 23:19; see also 24:3(5)). 6. Is not amendable. 7. Is normally ruled upon by the chair. No vote is taken unless the chair is in doubt or his ruling is appealed.

turns.speaker: Member (Grant)
turns.line: Madam Chair, when you rule on this, are you bound to follow the way you ruled on a nearly identical objection two meetings ago?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Mr. Grant asks how much a prior ruling controls this one.
turns.prompt: The chair asks you: what standing does a prior ruling have as a precedent?
turns.gold: A ruling and its recorded rationale serve as a precedent for the chair and the assembly, unless it was overturned on appeal. Such precedents are persuasive when similar issues arise, carrying weight absent overriding reasons to depart, but they are not binding on the chair or the assembly.

ref.quote: The ruling and its rationale serve as a precedent for future reference by the chair and the assembly, unless overturned on appeal, the result of which is also recorded in the minutes and may create a contrary precedent. When similar issues arise in the future, such precedents are persuasive in resolving them—that is, they carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course—but they are not binding on the chair or the assembly.

turns.speaker: Member (Grant)
turns.line: Suppose the Council decides that old precedent was wrong. How do we actually get rid of it?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Mr. Grant asks how an unsatisfactory precedent can be superseded.
turns.prompt: The chair turns to you: how may the Council overrule or displace an existing precedent?
turns.gold: A precedent may be overruled in whole or in part by a later ruling of the chair, or by a decision of the assembly on appeal in a similar situation, which then creates a new precedent. Alternatively, the Council can adopt, rescind, or amend a bylaw provision, special rule of order, standing rule, or other motion to change the underlying rule or policy on which the precedent rested.

ref.quote: If an assembly is or becomes dissatisfied with a precedent, it may be overruled, in whole or in part, by a later ruling of the chair or a decision of the assembly in an appeal in a similar situation, which will then create a new precedent. Alternatively, adoption, rescission, or amendment (35) of a bylaw provision, special rule of order, standing rule, or other motion may alter the rule or policy on which the unsatisfactory precedent was based.

title: Appeals at the Harborview Association
setting: The Harborview Association is considering a contested amendment. The chair has been making rulings, and members test the limits of their right to appeal. You advise throughout.

turns.speaker: Member (Ito)
turns.line: I appeal the chair's ruling that my amendment isn't germane—and I also want to appeal your point-of-order ruling that came up while my first appeal was pending.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Mr. Ito wants to appeal a ruling on a point of order that arose while his appeal was already pending.
turns.prompt: The chair asks you: are there rulings from which no appeal may be taken, and does this situation fall within them?
turns.gold: Yes. There is no appeal from the chair's decision on a point of order raised while an appeal is already pending, so Mr. Ito's second appeal is not allowed. An appeal is also barred where the ruling concerns a question about which there cannot possibly be two reasonable opinions, since such an appeal would be dilatory.

ref.quote: Can be applied to any ruling by the presiding officer except that: a) if a point of order is raised while an appeal is pending, there is no appeal from the chair’s decision on this point of order, although the correctness of the ruling can be brought up later by a motion covering the case; and b) when the chair rules on a question about which there cannot possibly be two reasonable opinions, an appeal would be dilatory and is not allowed.

turns.speaker: Member (Ito)
turns.line: Fine—then I'll appeal the germaneness ruling itself. And I insist that appeal be debated fully.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Mr. Ito appeals the germaneness ruling and demands debate on it.
turns.prompt: The chair asks you: is this appeal debatable, and what are the exceptions?
turns.gold: This appeal is debatable, so Mr. Ito may debate it, subject to the rule that no one but the chair speaks more than once. Debate would not be allowed if the appeal related to indecorum or a transgression of the rules of speaking, to the priority of business, or if an undebatable question were immediately pending or involved; none of those applies to a germaneness ruling, so the appeal is debatable.

ref.quote: Is debatable, unless it (a) relates to indecorum or a transgression of the rules of speaking; (b) relates to the priority of business; or (c) is made when an undebatable question is immediately pending or involved in the appeal.

turns.speaker: Member (Jain)
turns.line: Earlier I asked the chair whether my motion would be in order, and she said no. I'd like to appeal that answer.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Mr. Jain wants to appeal the chair's answer to his earlier parliamentary inquiry.
turns.prompt: The chair turns to you: may the chair's response to that inquiry be appealed?
turns.gold: No. The chair's response to a parliamentary inquiry or other query is an opinion, not a ruling on a question that has actually arisen, and no appeal lies from it. If Mr. Jain wants the point at issue, he may make the motion despite the opinion; then, if the chair rules it out of order, that ruling can be appealed.

ref.quote: No appeal can be made from the chair’s response to a parliamentary inquiry or other query, since such a reply is an opinion rendered by the chair, not a ruling on a question that has actually arisen.

turns.speaker: Member (Ito)
turns.line: So my germaneness appeal stands. What exactly does the chair do now to process it?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The chair asks how to put the pending appeal to the assembly.
turns.prompt: The chair asks you: describe the procedure for handling the appeal now that it is made and seconded.
turns.gold: Having been seconded, the appeal takes the question from the chair to the assembly. The chair states the exact question at issue and puts it as "Shall the decision of the chair be sustained?", so that an affirmative vote sustains the ruling. The question is on the decision, not on the presiding officer, and after the vote is announced business resumes according to the result.

ref.quote: The question is not on “sustaining the chair,” because the decision, not the presiding officer, is in question.

title: Suspending the Rules at the Summit Society
setting: The Summit Society wants to move quickly through a full agenda. Members repeatedly move to suspend the rules for various purposes, and you advise the chair on what may and may not be suspended and by what vote.

turns.speaker: Member (Keller)
turns.line: I move to suspend the rules so we can seat three new directors tonight without the two-week notice our bylaws require.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Mr. Keller moves to suspend a bylaw notice requirement.
turns.prompt: The chair asks you: may the Society suspend that bylaw provision?
turns.gold: No. Rules contained in the bylaws cannot be suspended, no matter how large the favorable vote or how inconvenient the rule, unless the particular rule provides for its own suspension or is in the nature of a rule of order. A bylaw notice requirement of this kind cannot be suspended, so the motion is out of order.

ref.quote: Rules contained in the bylaws (or constitution) cannot be suspended—no matter how large the vote in favor of doing so or how inconvenient the rule in question may be—unless the particular rule specifically provides for its own suspension, or unless the rule properly is in the nature of a rule of order as described in 2:14.

turns.speaker: Member (Lund)
turns.line: Then I move to suspend the rules so we can debate both the budget and the dues increase at the same time to save time.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Mr. Lund moves to suspend the rules so two main questions can be considered together.
turns.prompt: The chair turns to you: can that rule be suspended?
turns.gold: No. The requirement that only one question be considered at a time embodies a fundamental principle of parliamentary law, and rules embodying such fundamental principles cannot be suspended even by a unanimous vote. The chair should rule the motion out of order.

ref.quote: Rules which embody fundamental principles of parliamentary law, such as the rule that allows only one question to be considered at a time (5:4), cannot be suspended, even by a unanimous vote.

turns.speaker: Member (Keller)
turns.line: All right—I move to suspend our special rule of order limiting each speaker to three minutes, just for the budget discussion. A bare majority is enough, right?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Mr. Keller moves to suspend a special rule of order and claims a majority suffices.
turns.prompt: The chair asks you: what vote is required to suspend that rule?
turns.gold: A two-thirds vote is required, not a majority. The three-minute limit is a rule of parliamentary procedure, and suspending such rules of order takes two thirds. In any case, no rule protecting a minority of a given size can be suspended against a negative vote as large as that protected minority.

ref.quote: Usually requires a two-thirds vote (see below, however). In any case, no rule protecting a minority of a particular size can be suspended in the face of a negative vote as large as the minority protected by the rule.

turns.speaker: Member (Lund)
turns.line: I move to suspend the rules. I won't say which rule—just trust me, it's needed.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Mr. Lund moves to suspend the rules without stating any purpose.
turns.prompt: The chair turns to you: is that a proper motion, and how must it be framed?
turns.gold: The motion is improperly framed. The specific rule need not be named, but the motion must state its specific purpose, and its adoption permits nothing else to be done under the suspension. The chair should ask Mr. Lund to state the object of the suspension before the motion can be entertained.

ref.quote: In making the incidental motion to Suspend the Rules, the particular rule or rules to be suspended are not mentioned; but the motion must state its specific purpose, and its adoption permits nothing else to be done under the suspension.

title: Parliamentary Inquiries at the Meadowbrook Club
setting: A newer member of the Meadowbrook Club is unsure of procedure during a pending motion. You advise the chair on how to treat parliamentary inquiries.

turns.speaker: Member (Nunez)
turns.line: A parliamentary inquiry, please! I know Ms. Park has the floor, but I need to know before she finishes—does my inquiry need a second, and can I raise it now?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Mr. Nunez rises to a parliamentary inquiry while another member has the floor.
turns.prompt: The chair asks you: may he raise it now, and does it need a second?
turns.gold: Yes, he may raise it now. A parliamentary inquiry is in order even when another member has the floor if it requires immediate attention, and it does not require a second. The chair should direct him to state the inquiry.

ref.quote: 3. All are in order when another has the floor if they require immediate attention. 4. A Parliamentary Inquiry and a Request for Information do not require a second. The other requests do not require a second, except when moved formally by the maker of the request. A motion to grant the request of another member does not require a second, since two members already wish the question to come up—the maker of the request and the maker of the motion.

turns.speaker: Member (Nunez)
turns.line: My inquiry is this: is it in order for me to move the previous question right now?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Mr. Nunez has stated a genuine parliamentary inquiry about the current situation.
turns.prompt: The chair turns to you: what is a parliamentary inquiry for, and must the chair answer it?
turns.gold: A parliamentary inquiry is a question to the presiding officer seeking information on parliamentary law or the organization's rules bearing on the business at hand. It is the chair's duty to answer when doing so may help a member make an appropriate motion, raise a proper point of order, or understand the situation. The chair should answer, though she is not obliged to answer hypothetical questions.

ref.quote: A Parliamentary Inquiry is a question directed to the presiding officer to obtain information on a matter of parliamentary law or the rules of the organization bearing on the business at hand. It is the chair’s duty to answer such questions when it may assist a member to make an appropriate motion, raise a proper point of order, or understand the parliamentary situation or the effect of a motion. The chair is not obliged to answer hypothetical questions.

title: Requests for Information at the Cedar Falls Guild
setting: During debate on a costly capital purchase, members seek facts rather than parliamentary guidance. You advise the chair on requests for information and on keeping them from being misused.

turns.speaker: Member (Ortiz)
turns.line: I have a request for information: this purchase is large—will the Treasurer tell us the current balance in the building fund?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Mr. Ortiz seeks a factual figure from the Treasurer.
turns.prompt: The chair asks you: what is a request for information, and how does the chair handle it?
turns.gold: A request for information, also called a point of information, is a request directed to the chair, or through the chair to another officer or member, for information relevant to the business at hand but not related to parliamentary procedure. The chair should direct the request to the Treasurer, who may answer through the chair.

ref.quote: A Request for Information (also called a Point of Information) is a request directed to the chair, or through the chair to another officer or member, for information relevant to the business at hand but not related to parliamentary procedure.

turns.speaker: Member (Reyes)
turns.line: I have a point of information too—and I want to amend it and debate it before the Treasurer answers.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Mr. Reyes wants to debate and amend his request for information.
turns.prompt: The chair turns to you: is a request for information debatable or amendable, and is a vote taken on it?
turns.gold: No on all counts. A request for information is not debatable, though it may be accompanied by brief explanation, and it is not amendable. No vote is taken on it. The chair should simply have the request answered, not entertain debate, amendment, or a vote.

ref.quote: 5. All are not debatable, although they may be accompanied by brief explanations as necessary (cf. 43:31–32). 6. All are not amendable. 7. No vote is taken on a Parliamentary Inquiry and a Request for Information.

turns.speaker: Member (Reyes)
turns.line: Then let me put my 'question' this way: isn't it true that this purchase is reckless and the board has mismanaged our money for years?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Mr. Reyes is using the request-for-information form to deliver an argument rather than seek facts.
turns.prompt: The chair asks you: how should the chair prevent this misuse of the request for information?
turns.gold: The request for information must always be put in the form of a genuine question; it may not be used to make a speech or argue a position under the guise of a question. Because Mr. Reyes is arguing rather than seeking information, the chair should require him to state an actual question and rule out the speech-making.

ref.quote: An inquiry of this kind may also be for the purpose of reminding a speaker of a point to be made in argument, or it may be intended to rebut his position; but it must always be put in the form of a question.

title: Rescinding a Past Action at the Grove Foundation
setting: The Grove Foundation wants to undo a landscaping order it adopted last year. You advise the chair on the nature of Rescind and Amend Something Previously Adopted and on the vote required.

turns.speaker: Member (Sato)
turns.line: I want to get rid of last year's resolution authorizing the extra landscaping entirely. What motion do I use, and what does it do?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Ms. Sato wants to cancel a previously adopted resolution outright.
turns.prompt: The chair asks you: which motion applies, and what is its effect?
turns.gold: She should move to Rescind, one of the two forms of a single incidental main motion that also includes Amend Something Previously Adopted. Rescind cancels or countermands a previous action, striking out an entire adopted main motion, resolution, order, or rule. Amend Something Previously Adopted would be used instead if she wished to change only part of the text.

ref.quote: By means of the motions to Rescind and to Amend Something Previously Adopted—which are two forms of one incidental main motion governed by identical rules—the assembly can change an action previously taken or ordered. Rescind—also known as Repeal or Annul—is the motion by which a previous action or order can be canceled or countermanded. The effect of Rescind is to strike out an entire main motion, resolution, order, or rule that has been adopted at some previous time. Amend Something Previously Adopted is the motion that can be used if it is desired to change only a part of the text, or to substitute a different version.

turns.speaker: Member (Sato)
turns.line: Does anyone need to second it? Can we debate it? And I noticed Mr. Tran was just assigned the floor—may I still give notice?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Ms. Sato asks about seconding, debate, and interrupting an assigned member.
turns.prompt: The chair turns to you: state the second, debatability, and floor characteristics of the motion.
turns.gold: The motion must be seconded and is debatable, and debate may go into the merits of the question proposed to be rescinded or amended. It is out of order when another has the floor, but previous notice of intent to offer it at the next meeting may be given after a member has been assigned the floor, provided he has not begun to speak—so Ms. Sato may still give notice.

ref.quote: 3. Are out of order when another has the floor; but, within the time limits specified in Standard Characteristic 1 above, a member can claim preference in being recognized for the purpose of making one of these motions when applied to a vote that has just been taken. 4. Must be seconded. 5. Are not debatable. (But see 43:31–32 regarding allowable explanation of an undebatable motion.) 6. Are amendable. 7. Require a majority vote, except a motion to close the polls, which requires a two-thirds vote. 8. The vote on a motion ordering that the polls be closed or reopened at a specified time can be reconsidered at any time before the order has been carried out. Otherwise, neither a vote to close the polls nor an affirmative vote to reopen the polls can be reconsidered; the same effect can be obtained by renewal or by the opposite motion. A negative vote on a motion to reopen the polls can be reconsidered within the period during which a motion to reopen the polls could be made originally. Other motions relating to methods of voting can be reconsidered. #### Further Rules and Explanation 30:4 Methods of Voting. In practice, the method of taking a vote usually can be agreed upon informally. But when different methods are suggested, they are usually treated not as amendments but as filling blanks, the vote normally being taken first on the one taking the most time. (For ways of voting, see 4:34–56; 45.) 30:5 A member who believes that a secret vote will give a truer expression of the assembly’s will on a pending motion can move that the vote on the motion be taken by ballot. An order that the vote on a main motion be taken by ballot also applies to a vote on whether to postpone the main motion indefinitely. (See also Roll-Call Vote, 45:45ff.) 30:6 Retaking a Vote. As explained in 4 (see 4:34–56) and 45 (see 45:11), the regular methods of initially taking a vote are by voice (viva voce), by rising (division), or by show of hands—the latter two of which may also be called for by any member as a means of verifying an inconclusive vote that has just been taken (see 29)—and the chair may order that a vote be counted or that an uncounted vote be retaken as a counted vote. After a question has been voted on in any of these ways, and within the time specified in Standard Characteristic 1 above, the assembly can still order that the vote be taken again by some method other than any of the regular ones (see Other Methods of Voting, 45:17ff.) or that an uncounted vote be retaken as a counted vote.
