turns.gold: No, a motion to take an informal straw poll is not in order, because it neither adopts nor rejects anything and so is meaningless and dilatory. If the board wants a non-binding sense of the room, it may instead go into a committee of the whole or quasi committee of the whole.

ref.quote: A motion to take an informal straw poll to “test the water” is not in order because it neither adopts nor rejects a measure and hence is meaningless and dilatory.

title: Voice, Rising, and the Division
setting: You are the parliamentarian at a chapter meeting, coaching a new chair through the standard voting methods and a demand to verify a result.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: We have a main motion to donate $200 to the food bank. It only needs a majority and there seems to be little opposition.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) I would like to take this the normal way. Give me the exact wording for putting this to a voice vote.
turns.prompt: Provide the standard script for putting the question to a voice vote.
turns.gold: Put it by voice: state the question, then say “Those in favor of the motion, say aye,” pause, then “Those opposed, say no.” You must always call for the negative even when the affirmative sounds unanimous.

ref.quote: In taking a voice vote, the chair puts the question by saying, “The question is on the adoption of the motion to [or “that”]… [repeating or clearly identifying the motion]. Those in favor of the motion, say aye. [Pausing for response.]… Those opposed, say no.”

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: That passed. The next motion is to amend our special rules of order, which I understand takes a two-thirds vote, and a voice vote on that would be hard to judge.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) What is the proper method for taking a vote that requires two-thirds?
turns.prompt: State the normal voting method for a two-thirds vote.
turns.gold: A motion requiring a two-thirds vote is normally taken by a rising vote, in which the two sides stand rather than voice their votes. The rising vote is also the method used to verify an inconclusive voice vote.

ref.quote: The simple rising vote (in which the number of members voting on each side is not counted) is used principally in cases where a voice vote has been taken with an inconclusive result, and as the normal method of voting on motions requiring a two-thirds vote for adoption (see Chair’s Announcement of the Voting Result, etc., below).

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: On a later voice vote I announced “the ayes have it,” but a member immediately called out “Division!” and no one seconded her.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) She had no second. Do I have to do anything, or can I move on?
turns.prompt: Rule on the member's demand for a division.
turns.gold: A single member may demand a division without any second, at any time from when the negative votes are cast until just after the result is announced. You must therefore immediately retake the vote as a rising vote so it can be verified.

ref.quote: Whether or not the chair pauses to say, “The ayes seem to have it…,” any member (without a second) has the right to require that a voice vote (or even a vote by show of hands) be retaken as a rising vote, so long as he does not use the procedure as a dilatory tactic when there clearly has been a full vote and there can be no reasonable doubt of the result.

title: The Ballot Vote and the Tellers
setting: The society's bylaws require officers to be elected by ballot. You guide the chair from the request to skip the ballot through closing the polls.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: It is time to elect the Secretary. Our bylaws require this election to be by ballot, and I have appointed tellers.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) A member moves that, since there is only one candidate, we suspend the rules and just take a voice vote to save time, and no one objects. Can we?
turns.prompt: Rule on suspending the bylaw ballot requirement.
turns.gold: No, when the bylaws require a vote by ballot that requirement cannot be suspended, even by a unanimous vote, so as to vote by a nonsecret method. The ballot must be taken even with a single candidate, and members may still write in other eligible names.

ref.quote: When the bylaws require a vote to be taken by ballot, this requirement cannot be suspended—even by a unanimous vote—so as to take the vote by a nonsecret method.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: We will ballot. The members have marked their slips and folding is complete.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) What exactly do I direct the tellers to do now, and what must they guard against as they gather the slips?
turns.prompt: Explain the tellers' role in collecting the ballots.
turns.gold: Direct the tellers to collect the ballots. As they collect, it is the tellers' responsibility to see that no member votes more than once, using whatever orderly method the society has fixed by rule or custom, such as a passed receptacle or a monitored ballot box.

ref.quote: When the balloting is completed, the chair directs the tellers to collect the ballots. In collecting the ballots, it is the tellers’ responsibility to see that no member votes more than once—for the assurance of which the assembly should adopt some reasonable and orderly method.

expected: no member votes more than once

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The tellers have made their rounds and no one seems to be still voting.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) How do I properly close the polls, and what if a latecomer then wants to vote?
turns.prompt: Provide the procedure and script for closing the polls.
turns.gold: Ask, “Have all voted who wish to do so?” and if there is no response say, “If no one else wishes to vote, the polls are closed,” which closes them by unanimous consent. If a member then arrives wanting to vote, reopening the polls requires a majority vote.

ref.quote: When everyone appears to have voted, the chair inquires, “Have all voted who wish to do so?” If there is no response, he says, “If no one else wishes to vote… [pause], the polls are closed,” thus in effect declaring the polls closed by unanimous consent.

title: Counting Ballots and Reporting the Result
setting: The polls are closed. You advise the chair and the tellers as they count, report, and consider a machine for future votes.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The tellers are counting in the side room. They found several slips turned in completely blank, plus a couple that were marked but show no clear preference.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Do those blank and no-preference slips count as votes cast when we figure the majority?
turns.prompt: Rule on how blank and no-preference ballots are treated.
turns.gold: No, the tellers ignore blank ballots and any others that indicate no preference, treating them as abstentions. They are not counted among the votes cast for purposes of computing the majority, since a choice needs approval by more than half of those actually expressing a preference.

ref.quote: In recording the votes cast, the principle followed is that a choice has no mandate from the voting body unless approval is expressed by more than half of those entitled to vote and registering any evidence of having some preference. Accordingly, the tellers ignore blank ballots and other ballots that indicate no preference, treating them as abstentions.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The chairman of the tellers is back and rises to give the count for Secretary.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Should the reporting teller announce who is elected, or is that my job?
turns.prompt: Rule on who declares the result of a ballot vote.
turns.gold: The reporting teller reads the tellers' report but never declares the result. The chair reads the report aloud and then declares the result, separately announcing the election of each officer.

ref.quote: The reporting teller never declares the result of a ballot vote. The result is always declared by the chair, who also reads the tellers’ report before he does so.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: For next year the board wants to speed things up with handheld electronic keypads instead of paper slips for these ballot elections.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Can electronic devices satisfy our bylaw ballot requirement?
turns.prompt: Advise on using electronic devices to meet a ballot requirement.
turns.gold: Yes, if properly authorized, but only if the devices meet the criteria for a ballot vote: members must be able to indicate their choices without revealing how they voted, and for an election they must allow write-in votes. Use for a ballot requirement must be directed by a special rule of order, standing rule, or a motion relating to methods of voting.

ref.quote: Their use to fulfill a ballot requirement in the bylaws may be directed in the same manner, provided that the devices meet the criteria for a ballot vote as stated in 45:18. Members must be able to indicate their choices without revealing how they have voted.

title: Nominations and the Election
setting: You are the parliamentarian guiding the chair through nominations for a multi-seat board and the moment an election becomes final.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Our bylaws are silent on how officers are to be nominated, and a member asks how we may go about it.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) What methods of nomination are available to us, and how do we choose one?
turns.prompt: State the available methods of nomination and how one is chosen.
turns.gold: The recognized methods of nomination are by the chair, from the floor, by a committee, by ballot, by mail, and by petition. Since the bylaws are silent and no rule has been adopted, any member may move to prescribe the method.

ref.quote: Methods of nomination are: (a) by the chair; (b) from the floor (sometimes called “open nominations”); (c) by a committee; (d) by ballot; (e) by mail; and (f) by petition. If no method of nominating has been specified in the bylaws and if the assembly has adopted no rule on the subject, any member can make a motion prescribing the method (31).

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: We adopted nominations from the floor. Three directors are to be elected, and one member has just tried to nominate four people at once for the board.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Can that member nominate four for three seats, and how should I manage repeated nominations by one person?
turns.prompt: Rule on how many persons one member may nominate for a multi-seat office.
turns.gold: No, in no event may a member nominate more persons than there are places to fill, so four nominations for three seats is out of order. Where several are to be elected, if a member objects, no one may nominate more than one person until every member wishing to nominate has had a chance to do so.

ref.quote: Where more than one person is to be elected to an office, such as to a board of directors or trustees, or to a position, such as to a committee, no one may nominate more than one person for the office or position, if an objection is made, until every member wishing to nominate has had an opportunity to do so. In no event may a member nominate more persons than there are places to fill.

expected: no more than there are places

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Nominations are still open, and a member wants to stand and speak in support of one of the nominees.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) May a member give a speech for a candidate, and what must happen first?
turns.prompt: Rule on debate and nominating speeches.
turns.gold: Yes. To give a nominating or seconding speech, the member must first be recognized by the chair while the floor is open for nominations. Once recognized, the member may nominate and speak for the candidate, or speak for one already nominated, but must avoid personal criticism of any candidate.

ref.quote: To give a nominating or seconding speech, a member must first be recognized by the chair while the floor is open for nominations. The member may then nominate a candidate and, without waiting for the chair to state the nomination, speak in favor of the candidate, or he or she may speak in favor of a candidate who was nominated previously.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: We have finished balloting and Ms. Ortega has received a majority for President. She is here in the room and does not decline.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) Is her election final right now, or does something more have to happen?
turns.prompt: Rule on when the election takes effect.
turns.gold: Her election is final immediately, because the candidate is present and does not decline. Had she been absent, it would be final only if she had already consented to candidacy, or otherwise once she is notified and does not immediately decline; after it becomes final the vote can no longer be reconsidered.

ref.quote: An election to an office becomes final immediately if the candidate is present and does not decline, or if he is absent but has consented to his candidacy. If he is absent and has not consented to his candidacy, the election becomes final when he is notified of his election, provided that he does not immediately decline.

title: What counts as a session at the Fairview Civic League
setting: You are the parliamentarian at the Fairview Civic League. Its bylaws set a regular monthly meeting, and tonight the chair is unsure how the evening's adjournment and next month's gathering relate.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Good evening. Before we start, help me get the vocabulary straight for the minutes.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (turning to you) A member asked whether tonight's gathering is a "meeting" or a "session." What is the difference?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what is the difference between a meeting and a session?
turns.gold: A meeting is a single official gathering of the members in one room or area with no cessation of proceedings. A session is the complete unit of business — one meeting, or a series of connected meetings, devoted to a single order of business. For most ordinary societies each regular monthly meeting is a separate, complete session.

ref.quote: A meeting of an assembly is a single official gathering of its members in one room or area to transact business for a length of time during which there is no cessation of proceedings and the members do not separate, unless for a short recess, as defined below.

turns.speaker: Member (Ortiz)
turns.line: So when we adjourn tonight, is that the end of it, or just a pause?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) If I simply declare us adjourned, versus adjourned sine die, does it matter?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: distinguish an ordinary adjournment from adjournment sine die.
turns.gold: An ordinary adjournment ends this meeting, and — since no continued meeting is set — ends this session too, but the League continues and meets again next month. Adjournment sine die means closing the session with no future meeting fixed, used to dissolve an assembly or close a body that will not convene again until a bylaw-prescribed time; it does not apply to an ordinary society's regular meeting.

ref.quote: The term adjournment sine die (or adjournment without day) usually refers to the close of a session of several meetings: (a) where the adjournment dissolves the assembly —as in a series of mass meetings or in an annual or biennial convention for which the delegates are separately chosen for each convention; or (b) where, unless called into special session, the body will not be convened again until a time prescribed by the bylaws or constitution—as in the case of a session of a legislature.

turns.speaker: Member (Ortiz)
turns.line: One more: someone wants to bar us from ever revisiting a decision at a future meeting.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Can this session lock in a decision so a future session cannot touch it?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what is the significance of the session for what a later session may do?
turns.gold: No. The significance of the session is the freedom of each new session: one session cannot put a question beyond the reach of a majority at a later session (except by a special rule of order or a bylaw amendment). A future session can rescind or amend what was adopted, so this meeting cannot permanently bind it.

ref.quote: The principal significance of the session as a complete unit of an assembly’s engagement in proceedings lies in the freedom of each new session, as contrasted with the limitations placed upon a session in progress by decisions it has made.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Clear. Thank you, parliamentarian.

title: Regular, special, and quarterly at the Harbor Yacht Club
setting: You are the parliamentarian for the Harbor Yacht Club, whose bylaws set a regular meeting on the first Monday of each month. The commodore has questions about a special meeting and about business carrying over.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: We need to act on a lease offer before our next regular meeting. (to you) How does a special meeting differ from our regular meeting, and what may we do at it?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: how does a special meeting differ from a regular meeting?
turns.gold: A regular meeting is the periodic meeting fixed by the bylaws, at which any business within the Club's objects may come up. A special (called) meeting is a separate session held at a different time and convened only to consider the business named in its call. So a special meeting is limited to the lease offer if that is what the call specifies.

ref.quote: A special meeting (or called meeting) is a separate session of a society held at a time different from that of any regular meeting, and convened only to consider one or more items of business specified in the call of the meeting.

turns.speaker: Member (Blake)
turns.line: We tabled the dredging project last time. Can it still carry over to a later meeting?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) How much time can pass between meetings before business can no longer just carry over as unfinished business?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what is the quarterly time interval and why does it matter?
turns.gold: Business carries over as unfinished business only if no more than a quarterly time interval separates the two sessions. No more than a quarterly interval has elapsed if the later session begins during or before the third calendar month after the month the earlier session ended. If more than that passes, the item does not carry over automatically.

ref.quote: In this book, it is understood that no more than a quarterly time interval intervenes between two sessions if the second session begins at any time during or before the third calendar month after the calendar month in which the first session ends.

turns.speaker: Member (Blake)
turns.line: At the special meeting on the lease, I'd also like to raise the parking rules.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) At the special meeting, may we take up parking too, and can we still amend the lease motion?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what business may be transacted at a special meeting?
turns.gold: No — the only business that can be transacted at a special meeting is what was specified in the call, so parking cannot be introduced unless it was in the call. However, that limit does not bar germane amendments or incidental motions arising from the noticed business, so the lease motion may be amended as if at a regular meeting.

ref.quote: The only business that can be transacted at a special meeting is that which has been specified in the call of the meeting. This rule, however, does not preclude the consideration of privileged motions, or of any subsidiary, incidental, or other motions that may arise in connection with the transaction of such business or the conduct of the meeting.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Understood. We'll keep the special meeting to the lease. Thank you.

title: Calling a special meeting at the Delta Rotary Foundation
setting: You are the parliamentarian for the Delta Rotary Foundation. The president wants to call a special meeting to approve an urgent grant, and needs to get the call and notice right.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) On what authority can I even call this special meeting, and what should our bylaw provision cover?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: on what authority may a special meeting be called, and what should the bylaws prescribe about it?
turns.gold: A special meeting may be called only as authorized in the bylaws (or by the assembly for a disciplinary trial). The bylaw provision authorizing special meetings should prescribe by whom such a meeting may be called and the number of days' notice required. If our bylaws don't authorize special meetings, we can't hold one.

ref.quote: A section of the bylaws that authorizes the calling of special meetings should prescribe: 1) by whom such a meeting is to be called—which provision is usually in the form of a statement that the president (or, in large organizations, the president with the approval of the board) can call a special meeting, and that he shall call a special meeting at the written request of a specific number of members; and 2) the number of days’ notice required.

turns.speaker: Member (Reyes)
turns.line: What exactly has to go into the notice we send members?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) What must the notice of this special meeting contain, and when must it go out?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what must the notice for a special meeting include?
turns.gold: The notice must state the time, place, and purpose of the meeting, clearly and specifically describing the subject matter of the business to be brought up, and it must be sent to all members a reasonable number of days in advance as the bylaws require. A regular meeting's notice differs: its date is already fixed by the bylaws, so the call need not restate a specific purpose.

ref.quote: Notice of the time, place, and purpose of the meeting, clearly and specifically describing the subject matter of the motions or items of business to be brought up, must be sent to all members a reasonable number of days in advance.

turns.speaker: Member (Reyes)
turns.line: A member also wants to give previous notice of a bylaw amendment for a later meeting.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Can that member give the required previous notice by having it sent out with the call, instead of announcing it at a meeting?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: may previous notice be given with the call of the meeting?
turns.gold: Yes. Instead of being given orally at a meeting, a notice can be sent to every member with the call of the meeting at which the matter will come up, where there is a duty or custom of issuing such a call. The member writes to the secretary alone, who then sends the notice with the call at the society's expense.

ref.quote: Instead of being given at a meeting, a notice can also be sent to every member with the call of the meeting at which the matter is to come up for action, in cases where there is a duty or established custom of issuing such a call.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Good — we'll get the call and notice out properly. Thank you, parliamentarian.

title: The annual meeting of the Summit Alumni Association
setting: You are the parliamentarian at the annual meeting of the Summit Alumni Association, which meets regularly all year. Elections are on the agenda, a sensitive personnel matter has come up, and some members joined by video.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) A member asked what makes this our "annual meeting" as opposed to any other regular meeting. How should I answer?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what are the two senses of "annual meeting," and which applies here?
turns.gold: "Annual meeting" has two senses: for a society that holds only one membership meeting a year, that meeting is the annual meeting; for a society like ours that meets regularly all year, the annual meeting is simply the regular meeting the bylaws designate. The only difference is that annual reports, elections, and any business the bylaws prescribe for it are in order, in addition to ordinary business.

ref.quote: In local organizations that hold regular business meetings throughout the year, however, the bylaws may provide that one of these regular meetings held at a specified time each year shall be known as the annual meeting. The only difference between this kind of annual meeting and the other regular meetings is that the annual reports of officers and standing committees, the election of officers, and any other items of business that the bylaws may prescribe for the annual meeting are in order, besides the ordinary business that may come up.

turns.speaker: Member (Nash)
turns.line: The personnel complaint is delicate — I move we close the doors and keep it confidential.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) How do we properly go into executive session, and what does it require of those present?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what is an executive session and how is one entered?
turns.gold: An executive session is a meeting, or portion of one, whose proceedings are secret; only members and those specifically invited may remain. The assembly enters it by majority vote on a motion to go into executive session (a question of privilege). Everything said is confidential and may not be divulged to nonmembers, and members can be disciplined for violating that secrecy.

ref.quote: An executive session in general parliamentary usage has come to mean any meeting of a deliberative assembly, or a portion of a meeting, at which the proceedings are secret. This term originally referred to the consideration of executive business—that is, presidential nominations to appointive offices, and treaties—behind closed doors in the United States Senate. The practice of organizations operating under the lodge system is equivalent to holding all regular meetings in executive session. In any society, certain matters relating to discipline (61, 63), such as trials, must be handled only in executive session. A meeting enters into executive session only when required by rule or established custom, or upon the adoption of a motion to do so.

turns.speaker: Member (Okoye)
turns.line: Several of us are on video tonight. Are our votes even valid?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Are the members participating by video validly part of this meeting?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: when is an electronic meeting valid, and what does it require?
turns.gold: Electronic participation is valid only if the bylaws authorize electronic meetings; without that authorization, business must be transacted at a meeting in one room or area. Even when authorized, the essential condition is the opportunity for simultaneous aural communication among all participating members — a group deliberating only in writing is not a deliberative assembly.

ref.quote: It is important to understand that, regardless of the technology used, the opportunity for simultaneous aural communication is essential to the deliberative character of the meeting. Therefore, a group that attempts to conduct the deliberative process in writing (such as by postal mail, e-mail, “chat rooms,” or fax)—which is not recommended—does not constitute a deliberative assembly.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Thank you. We'll proceed correctly on all three points.

title: Motions that keep getting ruled out at the Crestwood League
setting: You are the parliamentarian at the Crestwood Neighborhood League. A series of main motions is running into trouble, and the chair needs help deciding what is in order.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Members keep offering motions I'm not sure are proper. In general, what conditions make a main motion out of order?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what conditions make a main motion out of order?
turns.gold: A main motion is out of order if it conflicts with the bylaws or applicable law; proposes action outside the society's object; presents substantially the same question as one already decided in this session; conflicts with a motion still in force; or conflicts with a question already temporarily disposed of and still in the assembly's control. Those are the five conditions to check.

ref.quote: Below are stated a number of characteristics or conditions that cause a particular main motion to be out of order, and— where applicable—the alternative courses that are open for accomplishing the desired result: 1) No main motion is in order that conflicts with the corporate charter, constitution, or bylaws (although a main motion to amend them may be in order; see 35, 57); and to the extent that procedural rules applicable to the organization or assembly are prescribed by federal, state, or local law, no main motion is in order that conflicts with such rules.

turns.speaker: Member (Frost)
turns.line: My proposal is arguably outside our stated purposes — is it just dead?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) If a motion is outside our object, is there any way to consider it, or is it simply lost?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what alternative is open when a main motion is out of order as outside the society's object?
turns.gold: It is not automatically dead. A main motion outside the society's object is not in order unless the assembly by a two-thirds vote authorizes its introduction. The chair can put the question of whether it shall be considered; or, if the chair rules it out of order, any member can move to authorize its introduction.

ref.quote: A main motion that proposes action outside the scope of the organization’s object as defined in the corporate charter, constitution, or bylaws is not in order unless the assembly by a two-thirds vote authorizes its introduction. If a member offers such a main motion, the chair can put to the assembly the question whether it shall be considered; or, if the chair rules the motion out of order, any member can move to authorize its introduction.

turns.speaker: Member (Frost)
turns.line: A motion we voted down earlier tonight — can I just bring it up again now?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) May a member renew, in this same session, a motion the assembly already rejected tonight?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what is the renewal principle, and what are its exceptions?
turns.gold: No — under the renewal principle the assembly cannot be asked to decide the same or substantially the same question twice in one session. The exceptions are the motions to Reconsider and to Rescind, and amending something already adopted. The rejected motion can be renewed at a later session, or become a different question if materially changed.

ref.quote: Renewal of motions is limited by the basic principle that an assembly cannot be asked to decide the same, or substantially the same, question twice during one session—except through a motion to reconsider a vote (37) or a motion to rescind an action (35), or in connection with amending something already adopted (see also 6:25).

turns.speaker: Member (Frost)
turns.line: And this bylaw change I want — everyone keeps saying it needs "previous notice."

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) What does a requirement of "previous notice" actually mean in practice?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: what does a previous-notice requirement mean?
turns.gold: Previous notice means that an announcement of the motion — indicating its exact content — must be included in the call of the meeting at which it will be brought up. Alternatively, if no more than a quarterly time interval has elapsed since the preceding meeting, the announcement may instead be made at that preceding meeting.

ref.quote: A requirement of previous notice means that announcement that the motion will be introduced—indicating its exact content as described below —must be included in the call of the meeting (1:7, 9:2–5) at which the motion will be brought up, or, as a permissible alternative, if no more than a quarterly time interval (see 9:7) will have elapsed since the preceding meeting, the announcement must be made at the preceding meeting.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: That clears the logjam. Thank you, parliamentarian.

title: Serving as parliamentarian at the Grandview Convention
setting: You are the professional parliamentarian at the Grandview Federation convention. The presiding officer relies on you to find rules fast, handle a disorderly delegate, and advise correctly during the session.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (quietly, to you) A tricky point just came up and I need the rule immediately. How do you find and apply the right rule so fast?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: how do you quickly find, explain, and apply the governing rule?
turns.gold: I work from RONR's structure: each motion's section has a standardized treatment — including a Form and Example subsection that scripts the exact words for the member and the chair — plus the tables and index, so the applicable rule can be located and applied quickly. I find the controlling paragraph, state the rule plainly, and give you the words to use.

ref.quote: Below for the main motion, and in each of the succeeding sections covering the different motions of parliamentary law (11–37), is a subsection headed Form and Example giving a script showing the words to be spoken by a member making the motion and by the chair in stating and putting the question on it.

turns.speaker: Delegate (Marsh)
turns.line: This resolution is garbage and its sponsor is a crook!

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) That was over the line. How should I handle this breach of order?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: how should the chair deal with this breach of order in debate?
turns.gold: For a slight breach the chair simply raps lightly, points out the fault, and advises the member to avoid it, and the member may then continue. Here the personal attack is more serious, so warn the delegate to confine remarks to the measure; if it persists, the chair or any member may call the member to order, and for a grave or obstinate breach the chair may "name" the offender.

ref.quote: If a member commits only a slight breach of order—such as addressing another member instead of the chair in debate, or, in a single instance, failing to confine his remarks to the merits of the pending question—the chair simply raps lightly, points out the fault, and advises the member to avoid it.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (quietly, to you) During the session, how and when should you be giving me advice — out loud, or only when I ask?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair: how should the parliamentarian give advice during a meeting?
turns.gold: During a meeting my work is limited to advising you — and, when requested, any other member — as inconspicuously as possible, usually quietly at your side. I should not wait until asked; if I see a problem developing I should raise it, even if it means asking the assembly to stand at ease for a brief consultation. You, not I, then state any ruling to the assembly.

ref.quote: During a meeting the work of the parliamentarian should be limited to giving advice to the chair and, when requested, to any other member.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Very good. I'll make the ruling. Thank you, parliamentarian.

title: Designing a members' training at the Westside Council
setting: The Westside Council has engaged you, a professional parliamentarian, to plan and teach a workshop on parliamentary procedure for its officers. The program chair asks how you'll build and evaluate it.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) These are busy adult officers, not students. What teaching principles should shape the workshop?
turns.prompt: Advise the program chair: what adult-learning principles should guide teaching parliamentary procedure?
turns.gold: I'll apply Knowles's principles of adult learning (andragogy): adults are self-directed, bring a wealth of experience to draw on, are ready to learn what's relevant to their roles, are problem-centered rather than subject-centered, and are motivated more by internal than external rewards. So I'll make the session practical, experience-based, and tied directly to their duties.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) How will you set the goals for the session so we know it worked?
turns.prompt: Advise the program chair: how should the lesson's objectives be written?
turns.gold: I'll build a lesson plan around SMART learning objectives — Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound. For example, "by the end of the session each officer can correctly state and put a main motion," which is specific and measurable rather than a vague aim like "understand motions."

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Walk me through how you'll actually prepare and deliver it.
turns.prompt: Advise the program chair: how will you prepare and present the lesson?
turns.gold: I'll prepare by setting the objectives, organizing content in a logical sequence, and readying materials and visuals. In delivery I'll open by stating the objectives, use active methods — demonstration and hands-on practice with real motions — keep the officers participating, and close with a summary and a check that the objectives were met.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Afterward, how will you judge whether your own teaching was any good?
turns.prompt: Advise the program chair: how should you evaluate your own teaching?
turns.gold: I'll evaluate by gathering learner feedback (written evaluations and questions), checking whether the participants actually met the objectives, and reflecting on what worked and what didn't. That evidence drives concrete improvements to the next session.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Excellent — that's exactly the plan we needed. Thank you.

title: An ethical dilemma at the Prairie State Association
setting: You are the retained parliamentarian for the Prairie State Association's convention. A board member raises questions about your professional obligations under NAP's Code of Professional Responsibility.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Where does this "Code of Professional Responsibility" you keep mentioning even come from?
turns.prompt: Advise the board: what is the background of the Code of Professional Responsibility?
turns.gold: The Code of Professional Responsibility is the set of ethical standards adopted by the National Association of Parliamentarians to govern the conduct of professional parliamentarians. It codifies duties such as impartiality, competence, and confidentiality, and NAP members serving professionally are expected to abide by it.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (quietly, to you) You're a longtime member here and you favor the amendment on the floor. Can't you just help our side win it?
turns.prompt: Advise the board member: how does the Code guide you in this ethical dilemma?
turns.gold: No. The Code requires me to serve impartially and avoid conflicts of interest, so I cannot use the parliamentarian's role to advance one side. I keep client confidences and give the chair objective advice; if I could not remain impartial, the proper course would be to disclose that and decline or withdraw.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) If a parliamentarian violated that Code, is it just words, or are there real consequences?
turns.prompt: Advise the board: what teeth does the Code have?
turns.gold: The Code contains fifteen enforceable provisions, and violating any of them is a disciplinable offense that can subject a member to NAP disciplinary proceedings, up to expulsion. They are genuine obligations, not mere aspirations.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: Understood. We appreciate your keeping this straight. Thank you.

title: Setting up your professional practice with the Cedar Ridge HOA
setting: You are launching a professional parliamentarian practice, and the Cedar Ridge Homeowners Association is your first prospective client. Its president wants to know how you run your business and structure an engagement.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Before we hire anyone, how do you even run this as a business?
turns.prompt: Advise the client: what goes into a parliamentarian's business plan?
turns.gold: A business plan defines the services I offer (meeting consulting, opinions, training), my target market and how I'll reach it, my pricing strategy, and realistic financial projections of income and expenses. It's the roadmap that keeps the practice viable and focused.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) What will this cost us, and how do you arrive at your fee?
turns.prompt: Advise the client: how should professional fees be set?
turns.gold: I set fees based on my experience and credentials, the prevailing market rate, and the scope and complexity of the engagement, structured as an hourly, daily, or flat rate as fits the work. I agree the fee with you in advance and put it in writing so there are no surprises.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) After you've done the work, how will you bill us?
turns.prompt: Advise the client: what should an invoice for your services contain?
turns.gold: My invoice will itemize the services rendered with dates, the hours and rate (or the agreed flat fee), any reimbursable expenses, and the total amount due, along with the payment terms. I send it promptly after the service per our agreement.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Do we really need paperwork for this, or can we just shake hands?
turns.prompt: Advise the client: how should the engagement be documented?
turns.gold: We should put the terms in a letter of agreement or contract before I begin — spelling out the scope of services, the fee and expenses, the timing, and each party's responsibilities — signed by both of us. A written agreement protects both sides and prevents misunderstandings.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: That's very professional. Let's move ahead. Thank you.

title: Prepping the chair before the Lincoln Township Board meeting
setting: You are consulting with the newly elected chair of the Lincoln Township Board ahead of a difficult meeting. She wants help running it smoothly and asks how you'll work together.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) I freeze up on the exact wording during motions. Can you help me not stumble?
turns.prompt: Advise the client: how can a script help her run the meeting?
turns.gold: Yes — I'll write you a script for the meeting that gives the exact words to open the meeting, take up each item in the order of business, and state and put each anticipated motion, including how to announce the result. Following a prepared script keeps the meeting moving and prevents procedural errors.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) How should the two of us actually work together so this goes well?
turns.prompt: Advise the client: what makes for a good working relationship with the parliamentarian?
turns.gold: We should set clear expectations up front and communicate openly, ideally conferring before the meeting and during recesses. I keep our discussions confidential and stay strictly in an advisory role — I advise, you decide and preside — and I keep you informed as issues develop so you can act at the right moment.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) When I announce results, does it matter exactly which words I use?
turns.prompt: Advise the client: why use standard parliamentary terminology?
turns.gold: Yes. Using correct, standard parliamentary terminology consistently — "the question is on the motion to...," "the ayes have it and the motion is adopted," "the motion is lost," "you are out of order" — keeps the proceedings clear and unambiguous and signals that the meeting is being run properly. Loose or invented wording invites confusion and points of order.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: That gives me real confidence going in. Thank you.

title: Handling bylaw amendments at the annual meeting
setting: The Oakmont Society is at its annual meeting. Two bylaw amendments have been noticed. You sit beside the chair as parliamentarian.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: We turn to the noticed bylaw amendments. Our bylaws contain no article on their own amendment.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) What vote and notice do we need to adopt an amendment when the bylaws are silent?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the default requirement to amend the bylaws.
turns.gold: If the bylaws set no amendment procedure, the default rule applies: the amendment needs previous notice and a two-thirds vote, or, as an alternative, a vote of a majority of the entire membership. You cannot lower that bar by ordinary motion.

ref.quote: Special requirements for this motion’s adoption should be specified in the bylaws, and they should always include at least notice and a two-thirds vote, which (with a vote of a majority of the entire membership as an allowable alternative) are the requirements for its adoption if such specification in the bylaws is neglected

turns.speaker: Member (Alvarez)
turns.line: The first amendment passed. My second amendment conflicts with the one we just adopted, so surely it is dead now?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Is the conflicting noticed amendment out of order now?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair whether the conflicting noticed amendment may still be considered.
turns.gold: All amendments for which notice was given are entitled to be considered, so this one is not out of order merely because it conflicts with the amendment just adopted. Take it up on the language of the bylaws as already amended.

ref.quote: all bylaw amendments of which notice was given are entitled to be considered, as a matter of the rights of their proposers, and a bylaw amendment is not dropped simply because it would conflict with one previously adopted

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: The vote on that second amendment was close, about 40 to 30.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) How should the result be entered?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on recording the vote on the bylaw amendment.
turns.gold: Because the vote was not nearly unanimous, the chair should have the vote on the bylaw amendment counted and the count recorded in the minutes.

ref.quote: The final vote on a bylaw amendment should be counted and recorded in the minutes unless it is nearly unanimous

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Next year the committee brings a full revision. How do we take it up?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the method of considering a revision.
turns.gold: A revision or a lengthy multi-section amendment is considered seriatim, section by section. Do not put the whole document to a single vote until the sections have been opened to perfection.

ref.quote: A revision of bylaws or a lengthy amendment involving more than one section should be considered seriatim as described in 28

title: Notice, scope, and form of bylaw amendments
setting: A special meeting of the Riverside Club has been called to consider dues and structural bylaw changes. You advise the chair.

turns.speaker: Member (Boyd)
turns.line: Dues are $10. I move to strike $10 and insert $25.

turns.speaker: Member (Cho)
turns.line: I move to amend that to $40 instead.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Is the amendment to $40 in order?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the scope-of-notice limit for amending a pending amendment.
turns.gold: An amendment to the pending bylaw amendment may not increase the modification beyond the scope of the notice given. Raising the dues figure above the noticed number is out of order, even by unanimous consent.

ref.quote: If the bylaws require previous notice for their amendment (as they should), or if they do not but notice has been given and a majority of the entire membership is not present, no amendment to a bylaw amendment is in order that increases the modification of the article or provision to be amended

turns.speaker: Secretary
turns.line: The mailed notice just said 'dues may be changed.'

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Is that notice sufficient, and how should notice be worded?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on whether the notice adequately informs members.
turns.gold: The notice is defective if it does not fairly inform the members of the change contemplated; a vague notice will not support the amendment. Restate it in the formal striking-out-and-inserting form.

ref.quote: The notice should fairly inform the members of the changes contemplated

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Members want the new dues to start next fiscal year, not today. How?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on drafting the transitional/effective-date provision.
turns.gold: Put the transition mechanics in provisos attached to the adopting motion, not in the bylaws themselves. The amendment takes effect immediately on adoption unless the motion to adopt fixes a later effective time.

ref.quote: An amendment to the bylaws goes into effect immediately upon its adoption unless the motion to adopt specifies another time for its becoming effective, or the assembly has set such a time by a previously adopted motion

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Do we need to vote on the new section headings and numbers too?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the status of captions, headings, and numbers.
turns.gold: Headings, article and section numbers, and captions are now an integral part of what the assembly adopts, not clerical marginalia. Only the assembly may change a heading if the change could affect meaning.

ref.quote: It is now the usual practice to include these subtitles or identifying numbers or letters as an integral part of what is adopted by action of the assembly

title: Which document governs, and special rules
setting: The Hillcrest Association's board is reviewing its governing documents. You attend as parliamentarian.

turns.speaker: Director (Diaz)
turns.line: We keep hitting a debate-length problem. Can we just adopt a house rule limiting speeches?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) What kind of rule is that, and how is it adopted?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on adopting a special rule of order.
turns.gold: This belongs in a special rule of order, which supersedes any conflicting rule in the parliamentary authority. Adopt it with previous notice and a two-thirds vote, or a majority of the entire membership.

ref.quote: Special rules of order supersede any rules in the parliamentary authority with which they may conflict

turns.speaker: Director (Evans)
turns.line: Someone wants to put our one-time office-move logistics into the bylaws.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Does that belong in the bylaws?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the appropriate content of the bylaws.
turns.gold: Temporary or transitional provisions do not belong in the bylaws; keep them out and handle them by proviso. Bylaws should carry only durable, substantive governance content.

ref.quote: Provisions of a temporary nature or relating to the mechanics of transition from old to revised bylaws should not be included within bylaws

turns.speaker: Director (Fox)
turns.line: Our charter says one thing and a bylaw says another about property.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Which controls?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on identifying the governing document.
turns.gold: In an incorporated organization the corporate charter outranks every other rule, and no bylaw may conflict with it. Look to the charter first; if the bylaw provision clashes with it, the charter governs.

ref.quote: In an incorporated organization, the corporate charter supersedes all its other rules, none of which can legally contain anything in conflict with the charter

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Should we even be incorporated?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the relevance of corporation status.
turns.gold: Whether to incorporate turns on needs such as holding property, making binding contracts, hiring employees, suing or being sued, and shielding officers and members from personal liability. Absent such needs, incorporation is generally not required.

ref.quote: Incorporation is sometimes necessary or may be advisable, depending upon the differing laws of each state, if the organization is to hold property, inherit a legacy, make legally binding contracts, hire employees, be in a position to sue or be sued as a society, protect its officers and members from personal liability, or the like

title: Interpreting the bylaws
setting: The Fairview League faces a dispute over an ambiguous membership clause. You are the parliamentarian.

turns.speaker: Member (Grant)
turns.line: Two of us read Article III completely differently.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) How do we even approach this disagreement?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on recalling the Principles of Interpretation.
turns.gold: This is a matter of interpretation, and the Principles of Interpretation apply. Advise the chair that the society decides the meaning of its own bylaws, guided by those established principles.

ref.quote: In preparing bylaws and interpreting them, the following principles of interpretation—which have equal application to other rules and documents adopted by an organization—may be of assistance

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Apply that here: the clause is genuinely ambiguous.
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on applying the principles to the ambiguous provision.
turns.gold: First, an ambiguity must actually exist; if the meaning is clear there is nothing to interpret. Where it is ambiguous, interpret it, if possible, in harmony with the other bylaws, and a majority vote decides.

ref.quote: If a bylaw is ambiguous, it must be interpreted, if possible, in harmony with the other bylaws

turns.speaker: Member (Hart)
turns.line: For years we've seated late-dues members even though the bylaws seem to bar it.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) That practice isn't written anywhere. Must we keep following it?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on when an established custom must be followed.
turns.gold: Where no written rule covers the point, the established custom is followed unless the assembly, by majority vote, decides otherwise in the particular instance. It has the force of a rule until then.

ref.quote: If there is no contrary provision in the parliamentary authority or written rules of the organization, such an established custom is adhered to unless the assembly, by a majority vote, agrees in a particular instance to do otherwise

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) I now raise a point of order: the custom conflicts with Article III.
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on custom that conflicts with a written rule.
turns.gold: Because the custom now conflicts with a written rule, a point of order raising the conflict makes the custom fall to the ground, and the written rule must be followed thereafter. Custom cannot override an express rule.

ref.quote: However, if a customary practice is or becomes in conflict with the parliamentary authority or any written rule, and a Point of Order citing the conflict is raised at any time, the custom falls to the ground, and the conflicting provision in the parliamentary authority or written rule must thereafter be complied with

title: When procedural law overrides the rules
setting: The Lakeside Cooperative, an incorporated association, is meeting. A statute governs some of its procedures. You advise the chair.

turns.speaker: Member (Ito)
turns.line: A statute clearly requires a mailed ballot, but our bylaws let us vote by show of hands.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Which prevails?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on procedural law versus the organization's rules.
turns.gold: Applicable procedural law prescribed by local, state, or national law binds the assembly, and any action taken in violation of it is null and void. The society's own rules cannot override that law.

ref.quote: Aside from rules of parliamentary procedure and the particular rules of an assembly, the actions of any deliberative body are also subject to applicable procedural rules prescribed by local, state, or national law and would be null and void if in violation of such law

turns.speaker: Member (Jones)
turns.line: We already adopted an action last month that violated that statute. Too late to object?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Is a point of order still timely?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the continuing-breach nature of the violation.
turns.gold: This is a continuing breach: action taken in violation of the rules of a continuing nature is null and void, so the point of order is timely even now. Adopting something in conflict with law is not cured by the lapse of time.

ref.quote: The only exceptions to the requirement that a point of order must be made promptly at the time of the breach arise in connection with breaches that are of a continuing nature, whereby the action taken in violation of the rules is null and void

turns.speaker: Member (Kim)
turns.line: Then let's just suspend the statute for today's meeting.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Can we suspend it?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on whether a procedural rule prescribed by law may be suspended.
turns.gold: A rule of order can be suspended, but an applicable procedural rule prescribed by federal, state, or local law cannot be, unless that law itself provides for its own suspension. The motion to suspend is out of order as to the statute.

ref.quote: No applicable procedural rule prescribed by federal, state, or local law can be suspended unless the rule specifically provides for its own suspension

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) How do I even know which authority controls a given action?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on finding the applicable procedural law.
turns.gold: Identify the governing procedural law by class: action conflicting with the bylaws, with a still-in-force main motion, with federal, state, or local law, with a fundamental principle, or with a rule protecting absentees or members' rights. Here the controlling authority is the applicable statute.

ref.quote: Instances of this kind occur when: a) a main motion has been adopted that conflicts with the bylaws (or constitution) of the organization or assembly, b) a main motion has been adopted that conflicts with a main motion previously adopted and still in force, unless the subsequently adopted motion was adopted by the vote required to rescind or amend the previously adopted motion, c) any action has been taken in violation of applicable procedural rules prescribed by federal, state, or local law, d) any action has been taken in violation of a fundamental principle of parliamentary law, or e) any action has been taken in violation of a rule protecting absentees, a rule in the bylaws protecting the secrecy of the members’ votes (as on a ballot vote), or a rule protecting a basic right of an individual member

title: Boards: authority, superior bodies, officers
setting: The Summit Chapter, a subordinate unit of a national federation, holds a board meeting. You attend as parliamentarian.

turns.speaker: Director (Lee)
turns.line: Can this board just start acting on its own on any matter it likes?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) What is the source and limit of this board's power?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the board's delegated authority.
turns.gold: A society has no executive board unless the bylaws create one, and the board holds only the power the bylaws delegate or the assembly refers to it. It cannot act beyond that grant.

ref.quote: A society has no executive board, nor can its officers act as a board, except as the bylaws may provide; and when so established, the board has only such power as is delegated to it by the bylaws or by vote of the society’s assembly referring individual matters to it

turns.speaker: Director (Moore)
turns.line: We passed a local rule that the national's charter seems to forbid.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Whose rule wins?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on subordinate-unit rules versus the superior organization.
turns.gold: A charter granted by the parent state or national organization supersedes any rule the subordinate unit adopts, because it requires the unit to adopt no rule conflicting with the grantor's. The local rule yields to the superior body's charter.

ref.quote: While such a charter is not an instrument of incorporation and is usually quite general in its terms, it supersedes any rules the subordinate body may adopt, because it carries with it the requirement that the subordinate unit adopt no rules that conflict with those of the grantor

turns.speaker: Director (Nash)
turns.line: Frankly, the chair keeps missing rules and leaning on you for everything.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) What is expected of the presiding officer here?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the presiding officer's responsibilities.
turns.gold: The presiding officer should be well versed in parliamentary law and thoroughly familiar with the bylaws and rules, even with a parliamentarian at hand. That duty rests on the officer, not the adviser.

ref.quote: This person should be well versed in parliamentary law and should be thoroughly familiar with the bylaws and other rules of the organization—even if he or she is to have the assistance of a parliamentarian

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Our ex-officio member from outside the society, may he vote?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the rights of an ex-officio board member.
turns.gold: An ex-officio board member who is not under the authority of the society has all privileges of membership, including making motions and voting, but none of the obligations, and is not counted toward quorum. Treat this ex-officio member accordingly.

ref.quote: If the ex-officio member is not under the authority of the society, he has all the privileges of board membership, including the right to make motions and to vote, but none of the obligations—just as in a case, for example, where the governor of a state is ex officio a trustee of a private academy

expected: right to vote and make motions

title: Board rules, business, conflicts, and turnover
setting: The Grove Foundation board, one-third of which rotates each year, meets. A contract vote is on the agenda. You advise the chair.

turns.speaker: Director (Ortiz)
turns.line: This board wants its own standing rules that differ from the society's.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) May we adopt board rules that differ?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the limits on a board's own rules.
turns.gold: The board may adopt its own special rules of order or standing rules, but only so far as they do not conflict with the society's bylaws, parliamentary authority, or rules. A conflicting board rule is void.

ref.quote: Such a board may adopt its own special rules of order or standing rules only to the extent that such rules do not conflict with any of the rules of the society listed above

expected: bylaws and rules of the society

turns.speaker: Director (Park)
turns.line: Half of us are traveling; can we just approve the contract by group email?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Is an email poll valid board action?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the requirements for transacting business.
turns.gold: A board can act only at a regular or properly called meeting with notice to every member and a quorum present. Polling members separately by phone or email is not board action.

ref.quote: a board can transact business only in a regular or properly called meeting of which every board member has been sent any required notice —or at an adjournment of one of these meetings—and at which a quorum is present

turns.speaker: Director (Quinn)
turns.line: I'm an officer of the firm we'd be contracting with, but I'd like to vote.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Should director Quinn vote on this contract?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on voting where a director has a direct personal interest.
turns.gold: A director with a direct personal or pecuniary interest not common to other members should not vote on the contract. Advise drafting the bylaw around that abstention rule; but note no member can be compelled to refrain.

ref.quote: No member should vote on a question in which he has a direct personal or pecuniary interest not common to other members of the organization

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) A third of the board just rotated off. What happens to their unfinished items?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the effect of periodic partial change in the board.
turns.gold: Because a fixed portion of the board turns over periodically, it becomes in effect a new board, and matters only temporarily disposed of fall to the ground, except those still in a committee's hands. Do not treat the old board's unfinished items as automatically carried over.

ref.quote: In cases where a board is constituted so that a specified portion of its membership is chosen periodically (as, for example, where one third of the board is elected annually for three-year terms), it becomes, in effect, a new board each time such a group assumes board membership

title: Committees: instructions, vacancies, authority, terms
setting: The assembly of the Delta Society has referred a matter to a committee. You are the parliamentarian.

turns.speaker: Member (Reyes)
turns.line: We already referred the venue question to the Events Committee. Can we still tell it what to do?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) May we send further instructions now?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on giving instructions to a committee.
turns.gold: Yes: after referral and before the committee reports, the assembly may give it additional instructions by majority vote, even at a later session. The instructions bind the committee.

ref.quote: After a question has been referred to a committee and at any time before the committee submits its report, even at another session, the assembly by a majority vote can give the committee additional instructions in reference to the referred question

turns.speaker: Member (Singh)
turns.line: One committee member resigned. Who fills the seat?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) How is the vacancy filled?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the power to fill a committee vacancy.
turns.gold: The power to appoint a committee carries the power to fill any vacancy in it. The appointing authority, not the committee, fills the seat.

ref.quote: The power to appoint a committee includes the power to fill any vacancy that may arise in it

turns.speaker: Member (Tran)
turns.line: Can the committee just sign the venue contract itself?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) What are the limits on this committee's authority?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the limits of committee authority.
turns.gold: A committee generally has less authority to act independently than a board; unless empowered, it may only report findings or recommendations. It cannot act for the society without specific authorization.

ref.quote: Generally the term committee implies that, within the area of its assigned responsibilities, the committee has less authority to act independently for the society (or other constituting power) than a board is usually understood to have

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Is the Events Committee standing or special, and when does it end?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the terms of standing versus special committees.
turns.gold: Distinguish the two types: a standing committee has continuing existence, while a special committee dissolves once its assigned task is complete. This body is special, so it ends on final report.

ref.quote: Ordinary committees are of two types—standing committees (which have a continuing existence) and special committees (which go out of existence as soon as they have completed a specified task

title: Appointing a committee
setting: The Bayview Guild must appoint a special committee. No method is prescribed in its bylaws. You advise the chair.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: We need to appoint the fundraising committee. Our bylaws are silent on how.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) What are our options for appointing it?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the methods of appointing committees.
turns.gold: When the method is not prescribed, the assembly chooses among the recognized methods, voted in order: election by ballot, nominations from the floor with viva-voce election, nomination by the chair, appointment by the chair, and appointment by a motion naming the members.

ref.quote: if different methods are suggested or moved, they are voted on in the following order: (1) election by ballot; (2) nominations from the floor (“open nominations”) with viva-voce election; (3) nominations by the chair; and (4) appointment by the chair

turns.speaker: Member (Ulloa)
turns.line: I'd like to nominate an outside fundraising expert who isn't a member.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) May a non-member be appointed?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on appointing non-members to a committee.
turns.gold: Non-members may be appointed to a committee, even as chairman, but control over each such appointment is reserved to the assembly in the individual case. The chair cannot seat a non-member without that approval.

ref.quote: It is possible for persons who are not members of the assembly or the society to be appointed to committees—even to the position of committee chairman—but control over each such appointment is reserved to the assembly in the individual case

turns.speaker: Member (Vance)
turns.line: This committee is just to carry out the fundraiser we already voted for.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) How should we compose it?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on considerations in appointing the committee's members.
turns.gold: A committee formed to carry out an order the assembly has already adopted should be small and composed only of members who favor the action. Anyone opposed should ask to be excused.

ref.quote: When a special committee is appointed to implement an order of the assembly, it should be small and should consist only of those in favor of the action to be carried out

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) When the committee deliberates, may interested members sit in?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on committee hearings and who may attend.
turns.gold: Before making substantive recommendations, the committee should give members a chance to appear at a hearing, but during its actual deliberations only committee members have the right to be present. So exclude the non-members while the committee deliberates.

ref.quote: When a committee is to make substantive recommendations or decisions on an important matter, it should give members of the society an opportunity to appear before it and present their views on the subject at a time scheduled by the committee. Such a meeting is usually called a hearing. During actual deliberations of the committee, only committee members have the right to be present

title: Small-board and committee procedure
setting: The Cedar Board has nine members present, and several committees are meeting alongside. You are the parliamentarian.

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: We're a small board tonight. Do we really need all the formality?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Which formalities can we relax in a small board?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the informal small-board rules.
turns.gold: In a board of about a dozen or fewer, the informal rules apply: hands instead of standing, no second required, unlimited speeches, informal discussion with no motion pending, votes without a motion when clear, the chair need not rise to put a question, and a member-chair may debate and vote.

ref.quote: The rules governing such meetings are different from the rules that hold in other assemblies, in the following respects: 1) Members may raise a hand instead of standing when seeking to obtain the floor, and may remain seated while making motions or speaking. 2) Motions need not be seconded. 3) There is no limit to the number of times a member can speak to a debatable question. Appeals, however, are debatable under the regular rules—that is, each member (except the chair) can speak only once in debate on them, while the chair may speak twice. 4) Informal discussion of a subject is permitted while no motion is pending. 5) When a proposal is perfectly clear to all present, a vote can be taken without a motion’s having been introduced. Unless agreed to by unanimous consent, however, all proposed actions must be approved by vote under the same rules as in larger meetings, except that a vote can be taken initially by a show of hands, which is often a better method in small meetings. 6) The chairman need not rise while putting questions to a vote. 7) If the chairman is a member, he may, without leaving the chair, speak in informal discussions and in debate, and vote on all questions

turns.speaker: Committee chair (Wong)
turns.line: Do these relaxed rules apply in our committee meetings too?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Do small-board rules extend to committees?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on when small-board rules apply.
turns.gold: The relaxed small-board procedures apply in all standing and special committees unless the society instructs otherwise. So this committee may use the informal rules.

ref.quote: The informalities and modifications of the regular rules of parliamentary procedure listed in 49:21 for use in small boards are applicable during the meetings of all standing and special committees, unless the committee is otherwise instructed by the society

turns.speaker: Member (Xu)
turns.line: In committee, can I move the previous question to cut off debate?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Is limiting debate allowed in committee?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on limiting debate in committees.
turns.gold: Motions to close or limit debate, such as the Previous Question, are not allowed in committee, so this attempt to cut off discussion is out of order. The committee's matured judgment is not to be curtailed that way.

ref.quote: motions to close or limit debate (15, 16) are not allowed in committees

expected: close or limit debate not allowed

turns.speaker: Non-member (Young)
turns.line: May I inspect the board's minute book?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Does this non-member have a right to the minutes?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on access to board minutes.
turns.gold: Only members of the board have the right to examine the board's minute book, unless the board itself orders otherwise. The requesting non-member has no right of access absent such an order.

ref.quote: Only members of the board have the right to examine the minute book kept by the secretary, unless the board orders otherwise

title: Committee meetings and reports
setting: The Ashford Council's Finance Committee is meeting and will report to the assembly. You advise the chair.

turns.speaker: Committee chair (Zane)
turns.line: Only three of our seven members showed up. Can we act?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) Do we have a quorum?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the committee quorum.
turns.gold: The quorum in a committee is a majority of its members unless the assembly has set a different number. With fewer than that present, the committee cannot transact business.

ref.quote: The quorum in a committee is a majority of its membership unless the assembly has prescribed a different quorum

expected: unless a different quorum is set

turns.speaker: Committee chair (Zane)
turns.line: We're ready to report to the assembly. What form should it take?

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) What type of report is this?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on the types of board and committee reports.
turns.gold: Reports fall into two categories: annual or periodic reports of boards or standing committees, largely informational, and reports on single items of business arising during the year. Classify this one accordingly.

ref.quote: For convenience in the discussions in this section, reports may be divided into two general categories as follows: a) Annual or periodic reports of boards or standing committees are usually submitted in accordance with requirements in the bylaws, are primarily for information, and should summarize important work done by the board or committee during the year or other period covered by the report. They may also contain recommendations—which may relate to general policy to be followed by the organization, or may propose specific action by its assembly. b) Reports relating to single items of business arising during the year fall into a number of particular forms—which are described for the case of committees in 51:30ff. As will be seen from examples there, these reports can often be quite brief unless special circumstances or instructions to the reporting body call for a detailed presentation of facts

turns.speaker: Chair
turns.line: (to you) How should we present our recommendations?
turns.prompt: Advise the chair on preparing recommendations in a report.
turns.gold: Recommendations for immediate action should be grouped at the end of the report and cast as one or more proposed resolutions, so the assembly can act on them cleanly. The reporting body should draft the resolutions itself.

ref.quote: In any report of a subordinate board or a committee (of type (a) or (b) above), specific recommendations for immediate action by the parent assembly should be grouped at the end—repeating them if they have already been noted at separate places in the report —and should generally be cast in the form of one or more proposed resolutions
